0: <title>Effects of interactions between feeding practices, animal health and farm infrastructure on technical, economic and environmental performances of a pig-fattening unit </title>
A. Cadéro¹,2, , A. Aubry1, J. Y. Dourmad2, Y. Salaün and F. Garcia-Launay2
Superscript(1)IFIP - Institut du porc, 35651 Le Rheu, France; 2INRAE Agrocampus Ouest, PEGASE, 35590 Saint-Gilles, France
(Received 6 June 2019; Accepted 21 November 2019; First published online 3 March 2020)

European pig production faces economic and environmental challenges. Modelling can help farmers simulate and understand how changes in their management practices affect the efficiency of their production system. We developed an individual-based model of a pig-fattening unit that considers individual variability in performance among pigs, farmers' feeding practices and animal management and estimates environmental impacts (using life cycle assessment) and economic results of the unit. We previously demonstrated that this model provides reliable estimates of farm performance for different combinations of management practices, pig types and building characteristics. The objectives of this study were to quantify how interactions between feeding practices and animal management influence fattening unit results in healthy or impaired health conditions using the model. A virtual experiment was designed to evaluate effects of interactions between feeding practices, health status of the pig herd and infrastructure constraints on the technical performance, economic results and environmental impacts of the unit. The virtual experiment consisted of 96 scenarios, which combined chosen values of 6 input parameters of the model: batch interval (35 days and 7 days), use or non-use of a buffer room to manage the lightest pigs, feed rationing (ad libitum and restricted) and sequence plans (two-phase (2P), daily-phase (DP)), scale at which the feeding plan is applied (i.e. room, pen and individual) and health status of the pig herd (i.e. healthy V. impaired). Variance analysis was used to test effects of the factors in these 96 scenarios, and multivariate data analyses were used to classify the scenarios. Healthy populations obtained on average higher economic results (e.g. gross margin of 11.20 V. 1.50 €/pig) and lower environmental impacts (e.g. 2.24 V. 2.38 kg CO2-eq/kg pig live weight gain) than the population with impaired health. With 35 days batch interval and DP feeding, populations with impaired health reached gross margin similar to healthy populations with 2P ad libitum feeding and 7 days batch interval. Restricted, DP and individual feeding plans improved the economic and environmental performances of the unit for both health statuses. This study highlighted that health status of the pig herd is the main factor that affects technical, economic and environmental performances of a pig-fattening unit, and that adequate feeding strategies and animal management can compensate, to some extent, the effects of impaired health on environmental impacts but not on gross margin.
Keywords: virtual experiment, growing pig, individual-based model, batch management, health status

A. Cadéro¹,2, , A. Aubry1, J. Y. Dourmad2, Y. Salaün and F. Garcia-Launay2
Superscript(1)IFIP - Institut du porc, 35651 Le Rheu, France; 2INRAE Agrocampus Ouest, PEGASE, 35590 Saint-Gilles, France

3: The virtual experiment performed with an individual-based model demonstrates that feeding strategies and batch management which apply in growing-finishing units may compensate to some extent for impaired health status of the pig population. In all situations, daily-phase feeding and restricted feeding improve both economic and environ- mental outputs. Extending the fattening period in impaired health populations allows lightest pigs to improve the income from delivery to the slaughterhouse. The model is
included in a decision support tool, which will allow consid- ering such interactions for on-farm application.
4: European pig production faces economic and environmental challenges. Feed cost (FC) comprises 60% to 75% of total pig production costs, of which fattening feed comprises 52% to 76% (AHDB, 2016). Pig production systems also contribute to water eutrophication, acidification and greenhouse gas emis- sions (McAuliffe et al., 2016). Fattening pigs contribute 70%

of total phosphorus (P) and nitrogen (N) excretions on farrow-to-finish farms (Dourmad et al., 1999). Environmental and economic performances of pig production systems are determined largely by zootechnical performance, feed formulas and feeding plans (Dourmad and Jondreville, 2007, Dubeau et al., 2011, Pomar et al., 2014). Health challenges can decrease zootechnical performance (Pastorelli et al., 2012, van der Meer et al., 2016). Shipping practices and batch management can interact with health conditions and influence technical results (Rivest, 2008, Lurette et al., 2011, Ferguson, 2015). To our knowledge, however, no study has estimated effects of interactions between feeding practices, batch man- agement and health status of the pig herd on technical, economic and environmental performances of a pig-fattening unit.

Modelling can help farmers simulate and understand effects of changes in their management practices on the effi- ciency of their production system (Gouttenoire et al., 2011). Predictive growth models have also been identified as poten- tial tools to estimate effects of health challenges on animal performance (Pastorelli et al., 2012). We therefore developed a dynamic pig-fattening unit model that considers individual variability in performance among pigs, represents farmers' practices and estimates environmental and economic perfor- mances of the unit (Cadero, 2017; Cadero et al., 2018a). The model has successfully passed three evaluations: expert knowledge-based evaluation (Cadero et al., 2018a), sen- sitivity analysis (Cadero et al., 2018b) and comparison of predicted and observed data (Cadero et al., 2018c). These results confirm that it is an appropriate tool to test different combinations of management practices, pig and building characteristics and their effects on farm performance. The objective of this study was to quantify, in a virtual experi- ment, how interactions between feeding practices and animal management influence fattening unit results, in both healthy and impaired health contexts.

6: Description of the fattening unit model The fattening unit model is a discrete-event, mechanistic and stochastic (for biological traits such as pig feed intake (FI), growth potential and risk of mortality) model with a 1-day time step (Cadero et al., 2018a). The pig-fattening unit is composed of a pig herd, a farm management strategy defined by the farmer and the farm infrastructure. The pig herd is organized in successive batches of pigs of the same age reared in the same room during the entire fattening period, until shipping to the slaughterhouse. Each pig is represented using an individual-based model based on the InraPorc model (van Milgen et al., 2008), which simulates FI, deposition of body protein and lipids, and the resulting growth and nutrient excretion of each pig. A profile was defined to each animal which consisted in an initial BW (BWi), mean protein deposition (PDm), a shape parameter of the PD Gompertz function (BGompertz) and two param- eters (a and b) that describe its FI. Individual values of the five

parameters are set according to their mean vector, their covariance matrix and their distributions according to Vautier et al. (2013). The mean value for BWi was 30.0 kg for gilts and barrows, and mean values for a, b, PDm and BGompertz were 4.45, 0.0216/kg, 143 g/day and 0.0171/day for gilts, and 5.02, 0.0189/kg, 150 g/day and 0.0194/day for barrows, respectively. Farm management is represented by farmers' practices and a calendar of events for planning the tasks to perform. The practices include batch management, rules for allocating pigs to pens, feeding practices and slaughter shipping practices. Farm infrastructure is described by a number of fattening rooms, each of which contains a number of pens of a given size. A buffer room can be used at the end of the fattening period to extend its duration for the lightest pigs that have not reached the minimum slaughter weight (SW) (i.e. to avoid economic penalties in the French market context). The model calculates technical, economic and environmental results for each pig and then for the overall unit. Gross margin per fattening pig (GM, €/pig) is the main economic indicator in the model. It equals revenue from sale of pigs (Rev, €/pig) minus expenses related to their feed and the purchase of piglets. The premium per kg of carcass is calculated according to the French payment grid used by default, which associates a bonus or malus value to each pig based on its carcass charac- teristics (weight and lean content).

Life cycle assessment (LCA) is used to estimate environ- mental impacts of each slaughtered pig, considering impacts from the extraction of raw materials to the farm gate. The model calculates environmental impacts associated with the fattening period of each pig, including the processes associated with feed ingredients (Wilfart et al., 2016) and feed production and transport, emissions from pigs and from manure in the buildings and during manure storage. The spreading of manure was also considered but with system expansion approach: the emissions at the field linked to min- eral fertilization (which would have occurred if there was no manure) are subtracted from the emissions linked to organic fertilization. Thanks to this methodological choice, only the surplus or the economy of emissions is attributed to animal production. The functional unit is the kg of live weight gain produced over 1 year (11 batches of 35-day batch interval and 53 batches of 7-day batch interval). Therefore, the envi- ronmental impacts of producing piglets and breeding SOWS in upstream farrowing and post-weaning units are not included. We considered potential impacts of pig production on climate change (CC, kg CO2-eq.), eutrophication potential (EU, g PO4-eq.), acidification potential (AC, g SO2-eq.), cumula- tive energy demand (CED, MJ) and land occupation (LO, n2.year). More detailed description of the model and the LCA performed in it can be found in Cadero (2017) and Cadero et al. (2018a).

9: A virtual experiment was designed to evaluate effects of interactions between feeding practices, health status of the herd and infrastructure constraints on technical and economic results and environmental impacts of the unit.

The virtual experiment consisted of 96 scenarios, combining chosen values of six parameters of the model: interval between arrival of 2 successive batches on the farm (7 days V. 35 days), use or non-use of a buffer room, feed rationing (ad libitum V. restriction to 2.5 kg/day) and its sequence plan (two-phase (2P) V. daily-phase (DP)), scale at which the plan is applied (i.e. mean performance of pigs from the same room, same pen or individual) and health status of the herd (healthy V. impaired health). A feeding strategy combines a feed rationing plan, a feed sequence plan and the level at which these plans are applied. It defines the feeds and amount provided to the animals, and the rules to shift from one feed or amount to another (i.e. fattening duration, age, weight, cumulative quantity of feed consumed or quantity of feed distributed). This strategy is applied at the specified level taking into account the associated performance for the shifting: individual weight, average weight value for pigs in the same pen or average weight value for pigs in the same room. The healthy population is the same herd used in Cadero et al. (2018a, b) and Brossard et al. (2014). This pop- ulation of 1000 barrows and 1000 female ((Large White X Landrace) X (Large White X Pietrain)) pigs was generated using two average animal profiles (Brossard et al., 2014) according to the procedure described by Vautier et al. (2013). The impaired health population was derived from the healthy one by applying reduced FI and average daily gain (ADG) according to the meta-analysis of Pastorelli et al. (2012) and increased mortality observed in commercial farms. The degree of performance degradation was assumed to represent an average decrease induced by digestive bacterial infections, poor housing conditions or respiratory diseases (Pastorelli et al., 2012), corresponding to a 9.4% reduction in average daily FI and a 14.1% reduction in ADG. To obtain these reduction rates with the model, we applied daily and individually a reduction parameter to FI (8.9%) and PD (14.1%). These rates were obtained through trial and error testing until reaching the targeted reduction of average daily FI and ADG of the batch observed in Pastorelli et al. (2012). Mortality rates for the two popu- lations come from farrow-to-finish farms data of the 2015 French national network (IFIP, 2016). The average rate for all of the farms (2.65%) was applied to the healthy popula- tion, while the average rate for the 30% of the farms with the highest mortality (4.25%) was applied to the impaired health population. Each simulated batch contained 400 pigs, which is the mean batch size in French fattening units (IFIP, 2016). Intervals of 35 or 7 days between two batches were chosen because they are related to the contrasting number of batches on the farm (4 V. 20 batches, respectively), which vary in their shipping constraints, and number of fattening rooms (3 V. 17 rooms, respectively). These two options result in a different number of days available for fattening each batch (102 V. 115 days, respectively). A buffer room can be used at the end of the fattening period for up to 21 days. The buffer room has a capacity equal to 5% of the total capacity of the farm, that is, 60 and 340 places for the 35-day and 7-day batch intervals, respectively.

Pigs were allocated to pens based on their weight and were fed according to either their individual weight (Ind), to the mean weight in the pen (Pen), or to the mean weight in the room (Room). The restriction to 2.5 kg/day in the rationing plan (Res) simulates ad libitum distribution up to of feed per pig per day. Feed intake and nutrient requirements of female pigs were used as the reference for feed formulation and were estimated with InraPorc® (2006). Two feeds, A and B, were formulated on a least-cost basis according to the procedure described by Brossard et al. (2014). Briefly, it consists in formulating feeds that contain 9.75 of net energy (NE) per kg and meet at least 110% of the standardized ileal digestible (SID) amino-acid requirement for an average female profile at 30 kg BW (beginning of fattening) and 120 kg BW (target SW), that is, 1.13 and 0.55 g SID lysine/MJ NE, respectively. The level of 110% of the SID amino-acid requirement was chosen according to the recommendations from Brossard et al. (2009) and Brossard et al. (2014) who highlighted that meet- ing 110% of the average SID amino-acid requirement at the starting of each phase allows to keep the same feed conver- sion ratio (FCR) when moving from 2-phase to 10-phase feeding. These two feeds were then combined in adequate proportions to meet 110% of the amino-acid requirements at the beginning of each phase in the 2P feed sequence plan and each day in the DP plan. The 2P strategy was designed to supply 1.13 SID lysine/MJ NE from 70 days of age to 65 kg BW (Feed A) and 0.86 g/MJ NE from 65 kg BW until slaughter (combining Feed A and Feed B). The DP strategy contained a progressive daily shift from 100% of Feed A to 100% of Feed B.

The selling price of pigs was based on the French market price in February 2014 (1.314 €/kg carcass), and the premium per kg carcass was calculated according to the French payment grid based on the carcass weight range and lean meat percentage. Piglet value in the simulations was estimated according to the cost of producing piglets in February 2014 (62.10 €/piglet of 30 kg BW).
10: A virtual experiment was designed to evaluate effects of interactions between feeding practices, health status of the herd and infrastructure constraints on technical and economic results and environmental impacts of the unit.

The average rate for all of the farms (2.65%) was applied to the healthy popula- tion, while the average rate for the 30% of the farms with the highest mortality (4.25%) was applied to the impaired health population. Each simulated batch contained 400 pigs, which is the mean batch size in French fattening units (IFIP, 2016). Intervals of 35 or 7 days between two batches were chosen because they are related to the contrasting number of batches on the farm (4 V. 20 batches, respectively), which vary in their shipping constraints, and number of fattening rooms (3 V. 17 rooms, respectively). These two options result in a different number of days available for fattening each batch (102 V. 115 days, respectively). A buffer room can be used at the end of the fattening period for up to 21 days. The buffer room has a capacity equal to 5% of the total capacity of the farm, that is, 60 and 340 places for the 35-day and 7-day batch intervals, respectively.

Pigs were allocated to pens based on their weight and were fed according to either their individual weight (Ind), to the mean weight in the pen (Pen), or to the mean weight in the room (Room). The restriction to 2.5 kg/day in the rationing plan (Res) simulates ad libitum distribution up to of feed per pig per day. Feed intake and nutrient requirements of female pigs were used as the reference for feed formulation and were estimated with InraPorc® (2006). Two feeds, A and B, were formulated on a least-cost basis according to the procedure described by Brossard et al. (2014). Briefly, it consists in formulating feeds that contain 9.75 of net energy (NE) per kg and meet at least 110% of the standardized ileal digestible (SID) amino-acid requirement for an average female profile at 30 kg BW (beginning of fattening) and 120 kg BW (target SW), that is, 1.13 and 0.55 g SID lysine/MJ NE, respectively. The level of 110% of the SID amino-acid requirement was chosen according to the recommendations from Brossard et al. (2009) and Brossard et al. (2014) who highlighted that meet- ing 110% of the average SID amino-acid requirement at the starting of each phase allows to keep the same feed conver- sion ratio (FCR) when moving from 2-phase to 10-phase feeding. These two feeds were then combined in adequate proportions to meet 110% of the amino-acid requirements at the beginning of each phase in the 2P feed sequence plan and each day in the DP plan. The 2P strategy was designed to supply 1.13 SID lysine/MJ NE from 70 days of age to 65 kg BW (Feed A) and 0.86 g/MJ NE from 65 kg BW until slaughter (combining Feed A and Feed B). The DP strategy contained a progressive daily shift from 100% of Feed A to 100% of Feed B.

The selling price of pigs was based on the French market price in February 2014 (1.314 €/kg carcass), and the premium per kg carcass was calculated according to the French payment grid based on the carcass weight range and lean meat percentage. Piglet value in the simulations was estimated according to the cost of producing piglets in February 2014 (62.10 €/piglet of 30 kg BW).
11: A virtual experiment was designed to evaluate effects of interactions between feeding practices, health status of the herd and infrastructure constraints on technical and economic results and environmental impacts of the unit.

The virtual experiment consisted of 96 scenarios, combining chosen values of six parameters of the model: interval between arrival of 2 successive batches on the farm (7 days V. 35 days), use or non-use of a buffer room, feed rationing (ad libitum V. restriction to 2.5 kg/day) and its sequence plan (two-phase (2P) V. daily-phase (DP)), scale at which the plan is applied (i.e. mean performance of pigs from the same room, same pen or individual) and health status of the herd (healthy V. impaired health). A feeding strategy combines a feed rationing plan, a feed sequence plan and the level at which these plans are applied. It defines the feeds and amount provided to the animals, and the rules to shift from one feed or amount to another (i.e. fattening duration, age, weight, cumulative quantity of feed consumed or quantity of feed distributed). This strategy is applied at the specified level taking into account the associated performance for the shifting: individual weight, average weight value for pigs in the same pen or average weight value for pigs in the same room. The healthy population is the same herd used in Cadero et al. (2018a, b) and Brossard et al. (2014). This pop- ulation of 1000 barrows and 1000 female ((Large White X Landrace) X (Large White X Pietrain)) pigs was generated using two average animal profiles (Brossard et al., 2014) according to the procedure described by Vautier et al. (2013). The impaired health population was derived from the healthy one by applying reduced FI and average daily gain (ADG) according to the meta-analysis of Pastorelli et al. (2012) and increased mortality observed in commercial farms. The degree of performance degradation was assumed to represent an average decrease induced by digestive bacterial infections, poor housing conditions or respiratory diseases (Pastorelli et al., 2012), corresponding to a 9.4% reduction in average daily FI and a 14.1% reduction in ADG. To obtain these reduction rates with the model, we applied daily and individually a reduction parameter to FI (8.9%) and PD (14.1%). These rates were obtained through trial and error testing until reaching the targeted reduction of average daily FI and ADG of the batch observed in Pastorelli et al. (2012). Mortality rates for the two popu- lations come from farrow-to-finish farms data of the 2015 French national network (IFIP, 2016). The average rate for all of the farms (2.65%) was applied to the healthy popula- tion, while the average rate for the 30% of the farms with the highest mortality (4.25%) was applied to the impaired health population. Each simulated batch contained 400 pigs, which is the mean batch size in French fattening units (IFIP, 2016). Intervals of 35 or 7 days between two batches were chosen because they are related to the contrasting number of batches on the farm (4 V. 20 batches, respectively), which vary in their shipping constraints, and number of fattening rooms (3 V. 17 rooms, respectively). These two options result in a different number of days available for fattening each batch (102 V. 115 days, respectively). A buffer room can be used at the end of the fattening period for up to 21 days. The buffer room has a capacity equal to 5% of the total capacity of the farm, that is, 60 and 340 places for the 35-day and 7-day batch intervals, respectively.

Pigs were allocated to pens based on their weight and were fed according to either their individual weight (Ind), to the mean weight in the pen (Pen), or to the mean weight in the room (Room). Feed intake and nutrient requirements of female pigs were used as the reference for feed formulation and were estimated with InraPorc® (2006). Briefly, it consists in formulating feeds that contain 9.75 of net energy (NE) per kg and meet at least 110% of the standardized ileal digestible (SID) amino-acid requirement for an average female profile at 30 kg BW (beginning of fattening) and 120 kg BW (target SW), that is, 1.13 and 0.55 g SID lysine/MJ NE, respectively. The level of 110% of the SID amino-acid requirement was chosen according to the recommendations from Brossard et al. (2009) and Brossard et al. (2014) who highlighted that meet- ing 110% of the average SID amino-acid requirement at the starting of each phase allows to keep the same feed conver- sion ratio (FCR) when moving from 2-phase to 10-phase feeding. These two feeds were then combined in adequate proportions to meet 110% of the amino-acid requirements at the beginning of each phase in the 2P feed sequence plan and each day in the DP plan. The 2P strategy was designed to supply 1.13 SID lysine/MJ NE from 70 days of age to 65 kg BW (Feed A) and 0.86 g/MJ NE from 65 kg BW until slaughter (combining Feed A and Feed B). The DP strategy contained a progressive daily shift from 100% of Feed A to 100% of Feed B.

Piglet value in the simulations was estimated according to the cost of producing piglets in February 2014 (62.10 €/piglet of 30 kg BW).

13: Variance analysis (ANOVA) was performed on 16 technical, economic and environmental variables using a generalized linear model to assess effects of parameters, and the inter- action of population health status with the other parameters. Fixed effects of the factors were tested using each of the 96 scenarios as the statistical units. The mean results per scenario were obtained for one fattening unit over 53 or 11 batches depending on the batch interval that applied in the scenario (53 batches for the 7-day interval, 11 batches for the 35-day interval, corresponding to the batches simu- lated for 365 days and the end of the last batch). We used the stats package (V3.3.0) and the glm function of R software for this analysis.

Multivariate analysis was performed to classify the simu- lated scenarios (Husson et al., 2010). It consisted of principal component analysis (PCA) followed by hierarchical clustering (HC). In the PCA, parameters were considered as illustrative

variables, and variables were considered as active variables, which were then subjected to HC. The PCA and HC were performed using the PCA and HCPC functions of the FactoMineR package (Lê et al., 2008) of R software (R Development Core Team, 2016). In a first step, PCA of all 96 scenarios was performed using 10 active variables (Supplementary Material Figure S1). This first PCA revealed that population health status strongly separated the scenarios on the first axis and that the population with impaired health had higher variability among scenarios than the healthy one. Therefore, a second PCA (Supplementary Material Figures S2 and S3) and HC of the 48 scenarios for each population (healthy V. impaired health) were performed using the following active variables: FCR (kg feed/kg weight), lean meat content of the carcass (Lean, %), slaughter age (SA, day), percentage of pigs with a light SW in the payment grid (%LSW), Rev, GM, CC, AC and LO. Analysis of variance was then applied to variables from the classes obtained from the HC. We used a linear model to assess the effect of class (statspackage-V3.3.0 and function of R software). When analysing classes, an aggregated environmental impact (AEI) was calculated by averaging the five environmental impacts (CC, AC, EU, LO and CED), each expressed as a percentage of its respective mean value.

15: Variance analysis results Batch interval had a significant effect on all variables except GM (Table 1). The 35-day interval induced lower FCR, SW, FC, Rev and environmental impacts than the 7-day interval. Population health status had a significant effect on all variables except Lean and Pexc. Due to lower FCR and higher SW, the healthy population induced higher economic results and lower environmental impacts than the impaired health population. Feed sequence plan had a significant effect on all variables except percentage of pigs at adequate SW on the payment grid (%ASW) and %LSW. The 2P feed sequence plan had lower GM and higher Rev, FC and environmental impacts than DP. Feed rationing plan had a significant effect on all variables except %ASW and %LSW. The ad libitum feeding plan induced lower Rev and GM and higher FC and environmental impacts than Res. The scale at which the feeding plan was applied had a significant effect on all variables except %ASW, %LSW and Rev. Individual feeding (Ind) induced higher GM, lower FC and lower environmental impacts than Pen or Room (Pen and Room did not differ significantly). The use of a buffer room had a significant effect on all variables except Lean and GM, and induced higher Rev and environmental impacts.

In the 96 scenarios, GM and %LSW had the highest variability, followed by %ASW and Nexc (Figure 1). Overall, health status, batch interval and feed sequence plan explained most of the variance; however, their respective contribution differed depending on the variable (Figure 2). Health status contributed greatly (>50%) to variance in

FCR, SW, Rev, GM and environmental impacts and to a large degree (38% to 42%) to that in %ASW and %LSW. Batch interval contributed greatly (>50%) to variance in SA and Pexc and to a large degree (31% to 48%) to that in %ASW and FC (€/pig). Feed sequence plan contributed greatly (>50%) to variance in Nexc and to a large degree (34% to 36%) to that in FC and CED. Feed rationing plan contributed mainly (32%) to variance in Lean.

The interaction between population health status and batch interval was significant for 11 variables (Table 1, Supplementary Material Table S1). Health status had a more negative effect on FCR, Lean and SW but a more positive effect on environmental impacts with the 35-day batch inter- val than with the 7-day interval. The interaction between health status and feed rationing plan was significant only for GM, with higher GMin Res situations. Interactions between health status and the presence of a buffer room influenced % ASW and %LSW. All other first-order interactions with health status had no significant effect on variables' values. The inter- action between feed rationing plan and the scale of application had a significant effect on Lean, explaining 10.4% of its variance. The combination Res-Ind resulted in the highest ean value (61.1%), while Lean did not differ among ad libitum situations and Res-Room. Lean from Res-Room and Res-Pen combinations also did not differ significantly.

18: The HC separated the healthy population scenarios into eight classes, while based on the inertia gain between clustering steps, we retained six classes for the impaired health popu- lation scenarios (Table 2). For each population, classes differed mainly by batch interval and feed sequence plan, with a smaller discriminant effect of feed rationing plan (Figure 3). On average, and as expected, the healthy popu- lation had better technical, economic and environmental results than the impaired health population (Table 3). Classes' variabilities in GM, expressed as a percentage of mean GM of their scenarios, were large, ranging from 222% for the class with the highest mean GM (35-day batch interval, DP restricted feeding, 35dDPRes) to -9% for the class with the lowest mean GM (35-day batch interval, 2P feeding, 35d2P) (Figure 4A). Healthy population classes had higher mean GM than impaired health population classes.

Among healthy population classes, Res plans had higher mean GM than ad libitum plans. Within each feed rationing plan, classes with the DP sequence had higher mean GM than classes with the 2P sequence. Classes with the 35-day batch interval always had higher mean GM than classes with same feeding plan and the 7-day interval but signifi- cantly so only for the 2P-Res plan.

Among impaired health population classes, mean GM was the lowest for classes 7d2P and 35d2P, in which the feeding plan was applied at the Pen or Room scale. Among the other classes, mean GM was higher for classes with the 7-day batch interval than the 35-day interval.

(gross margin, percentages of pigs slaughtered at adequate SW or too light weight), which depend on many technical results and aggregate the variability in them. Indeed, gross margin is directly related to FC, which is influenced by the

feeding strategy, feed efficiency and revenue per pig, which are related to SW, carcass lean content and pig mortality. Percentage of pigs with a light SW in the payment grid had the highest variability because a group of 12 scenarios

was characterized by an average value of %LSW equal to 30%. These 12 scenarios corresponded to impaired health combined with 35-day batch interval and no buffer room. In this case, the maximal fattening duration is reduced rela- tively to 7-day batch interval, and there is no possibility to extend the fattening period of light pigs. In the case of impaired health, it results in the delivery to the slaughter- house of a large proportion of light pigs.

The amounts of N and P excreted also showed high variability, resulting in high variability in the associated environmental impacts CC, AC and EU. However, for EU it is worth mentioning that health status had an effect on N excreted and EU, but not on P excreted. Impaired health resulted in higher FCR what means that for the same weight gain more feed was needed. Consequently, since all environmental impacts were calculated according to LCA, EU impact was degraded by impaired health status through the increase of emissions and resources needed for feed production.

The variability in model variables is similar to that observed in the French fattening pig unit database (IFIP, 2016) for FCR (2.76 + 0.08 V. 2.88 + 0.28 kg feed/kg gain), Lean (60.2 + 0.7 V. 60.3 + 0.9%), %ASW (91.4 + 9.8 V. 86.0 + 6.8%) and Rev (127.10 + 5.80 V. 125.00 + 11.90 €/pig). Differences are observed, however, between predicted and observed values for certain variables, such as SW (114.9 V. 120.9 + 4.1 kg) and GM (6.40 + 5.30 V. 16.60 + 15.80 €/pig), which can be explained by the greater diversity in batch management and economic context in the French database (e.g. batch interval, pig genotype, health status and label). In addition, 50% of model scenarios represented impaired health status, while results of the French database are rarely associated with health status. Furthermore, the DP feeding and individual scale of applica- tion simulated do not currently exist on commercial farms and thus do not exist in the French database.

Variability in performance among scenarios is due mainly to the health status of the population, followed by the batch interval and the feed sequence plan. In simulations, health status is represented by growth, FI potential and mortality rate. In a previous sensitivity analysis, the last two factors had the largest influence on model variables (Cadero et al., 2018b). With reduced FI and growth potential, pigs with impaired health have higher FCR and lower SW than healthy pigs for a given fattening duration, which results in lower revenue and gross margin per pig, which are also influ- enced by mortality. The healthy population produced more kg live weight than the impaired health population, which reduced its environmental impacts per kg live weight gain. The two batch intervals created different constraints for fatten- ing duration, which influences SA, SW and the %ASW. This difference in fattening duration results in differences in total N and P excreted and FC, because the longer the pigs remain on the farm, the more they eat and excrete. Daily-phase feed- ing closely follows the needs of each pig and thus results in less nutrient excess and excretion. Nitrogen excretion directly influences CC, AC and EU and indirectly influences CED,
which explains the significant contribution of feed sequence plan to the variability in these impacts.

While health status has a major effect on technical, economic and environmental performances of the unit, management practices can also significantly decrease or increase these effects. For instance, the influence of the batch interval differs depending on the health status. In the healthy population, classes with the 35-day batch interval have higher gross margin and lower environmental impacts than those with the 7-day interval for the same feeding practices. Conversely, in the impaired health population, classes with the 7-day batch interval have higher gross margin but higher environmental impacts than those with the 35-day interval. In the impaired health population, the 7-day batch interval yields a longer fattening duration than the 35-day interval, thus increasing %ASW. Vangroenweghe et al. (2012) studied effects of chang- ing from a 7-day batch interval to a 28- or 35-day interval on the health status of the herd. They observed that health status improved significantly as batch interval increased (7 V. 28 V. 35 days), perhaps because increasing the batch interval decreases the number of simultaneous batches on the farm and the mixing of pigs from different batches. Increasing the batch interval seems to improve the health status of the herd, and accounting for this effect in the model would have needed the development of a whole epidemiological sub-model like the one of Lurette et al. (2008).

In the healthy population, restricted instead of ad libitum feeding increased lean content by 1.5% and gross margin by 27.4% (3.60 €/pig), and reduced FCR by 1.5%, N excretion by 3.8% and all environmental impacts (CC, AC, EU, CED and LO) by 1.6%, on average. In the impaired health population, restricted V. ad libitum feeding increased lean content by 0.9% and gross margin by 67.5% (1.50 €/pig), and reduced FCR by 0.9%, N excretion by 2.5% and all environmental impacts (CC, AC, EU, CED and LO) by 0.9%, on average. Brossard et al. (2014) showed that using restricted V. ad libitum feeding decreased FCR by 1.1% and N excreted by 3.6%, and increased lean content by 1.6%. Therefore, with restricted feeding, pigs reach the target SW later than with ad libitum feeding. Depending on the batch interval and the use of a buffer room, this may or may not result in a decrease in SW.

For the feed sequence plan, using a DP V. 2P plan increased FCR. Although we formulated Feeds A and B to meet 110% of the average requirement of the gilts at 30 kg and 120 BW, respectively (Brossard et al., 2014), when increasing the number of feeding phases, some of the most demanding pigs do not have their requirements covered at the starting of each phase. This is even more pro- nounced for DP feeding. Using a DP V. 2P plan decreased N excretion by 11.8%; P excretion by 4.0%; CC, AC, EU, CED by 4.8% and LO by 1.7%, on average, in both populations. It also increased gross margin by 15.2% (1.90 €/pig) in the healthy population and by 84.0% (2.20 €/pig) in the impaired health population. Daily-phase feeding follows nutritional

requirements of pigs more closely and reduces oversupply of nutrients, as mentioned, especially of amino acids and minerals. Pomar et al. (2014) showed that DP feeding reduced N excretion by 11.8% and P excretion by 1.9% com- pared to three-phase feeding. Nitrogen excretion was reduced by 30% when DP feeding was applied at the individ- ual scale compared to three-phase feeding applied at the room scale (Andretta et al., 2016). In our virtual experiment, DP feeding reduced N excretion by 12% compared to 2P feeding, and DP feeding applied at the individual scale reduced N excretion by 15% compared to 2P feeding applied at the room scale. The difference between the decrease in N excretion found by Andretta et al. (2016) and our virtual experiment is because our feeds were formulated to meet at least 110% of the mean SID amino-acid requirements of the population, while those of Andretta et al. (2016) were formulated to meet 100% of SID amino-acid requirements of each individual.

Last, interaction between feeding strategies and health status also occurred. Indeed class with 35-day interval and DP with impaired health resulted in the same average environmental impact values as class with 7-day interval, 2P and ad libitum feeding for a healthy population (Figure 4). This means that adequate feeding strategies and animal management can compensate, to some extent, the effects of impaired health on environmental impacts. Regarding gross margin, on the contrary, all scenarios with impaired health resulted in lower value than in healthy population.

Modelling, a possible alternative to animal experiments Modelling is a recognized alternative to animal experiments, with lower costs and fewer ethical problems (Doke and Dhawale, 2015). Models that represent livestock farming sys- tems can help research practices improve economic and envi- ronmental performances of farms (Gouttenoire et al., 2011). Our study tested effects of 96 combinations of factors, which would have been difficult to perform in vivo, given the num- ber of pigs simulated (4400 or 21 200 per scenario, depend- ing on batch interval) and the time needed per scenario (at least 105 days in vivo V. 2 min with the model). The modelling approach also helps to understand mechanisms responsible for differences in farm performance.

Modelling also appears an interesting approach to explore interactions with animal health status, which is difficult to address in experiments. Health status of the herd directly influences technical performance (Pastorelli et al., 2012). Impaired health increases the energy required for maintenance and modifies requirements for amino acids (van der Meer et al., 2016). Therefore, the health status of the pig herd requires adapting feeding practices to better fit these changes. Variability in the health status of pigs on the farm increases the variability in the pigs' requirements. Our study shows that applying DP and/or restricted feeding at the individual scale decreases the effect of impaired health status on economic and environmental performances. However, we used results from a meta-analysis based on

experimental data to simulate the loss in pig performance for impaired health status, through the average effect of a single disease. The performance damage is usually much more severe in field conditions, as several challenges can apply simultaneously and have additional effects. The effects of health status in interaction with other factors may be more pronounced than in our simulations. Ultimately, adjusting nutrient supply as closely as possible to pig requirements seems an appropriate solution to increase a farm's income, regardless of the health status of its pig herd. This assertion is consistent with previous studies (Pomar et al., 2010, Andretta et al., 2016, Monteiro et al., 2017).
19: The HC separated the healthy population scenarios into eight classes, while based on the inertia gain between clustering steps, we retained six classes for the impaired health popu- lation scenarios (Table 2). For each population, classes differed mainly by batch interval and feed sequence plan, with a smaller discriminant effect of feed rationing plan (Figure 3). On average, and as expected, the healthy popu- lation had better technical, economic and environmental results than the impaired health population (Table 3). Classes' variabilities in GM, expressed as a percentage of mean GM of their scenarios, were large, ranging from 222% for the class with the highest mean GM (35-day batch interval, DP restricted feeding, 35dDPRes) to -9% for the class with the lowest mean GM (35-day batch interval, 2P feeding, 35d2P) (Figure 4A). Healthy population classes had higher mean GM than impaired health population classes.

Among healthy population classes, Res plans had higher mean GM than ad libitum plans. Within each feed rationing plan, classes with the DP sequence had higher mean GM than classes with the 2P sequence. Classes with the 35-day batch interval always had higher mean GM than classes with same feeding plan and the 7-day interval but signifi- cantly so only for the 2P-Res plan.

Among impaired health population classes, mean GM was the lowest for classes 7d2P and 35d2P, in which the feeding plan was applied at the Pen or Room scale. Among the other classes, mean GM was higher for classes with the 7-day batch interval than the 35-day interval.

Figure 4 Effect of class of pig-fattening unit on (a) gross margin, expressed as a percentage of mean overall gross margin, and (b) environmental impact, calculated by averaging the five environmental impacts (climate change, acidification, eutrophication, cumulative energy demand and land occupation) per class, expressed as a percentage of their respective mean value. Classes are ranked from highest to lowest mean gross margin. 35 days = 35-day interval between arrival of two successive batches on the farm; 7 days = 7-day interval between arrival of two successive batches on the farm; 2P = two-phase feed sequence plan; DP = daily-phase feed sequence plan; Res = restriction to 2.5 kg/day rationingplan;Adl=adlibitumplan;Ind=individualscaleat which thefeedingplanisapplied = R + P = scenarios with room or pen scale at which the feeding plan is applied.

(gross margin, percentages of pigs slaughtered at adequate SW or too light weight), which depend on many technical results and aggregate the variability in them. Indeed, gross margin is directly related to FC, which is influenced by the

feeding strategy, feed efficiency and revenue per pig, which are related to SW, carcass lean content and pig mortality. Percentage of pigs with a light SW in the payment grid had the highest variability because a group of 12 scenarios

was characterized by an average value of %LSW equal to 30%. These 12 scenarios corresponded to impaired health combined with 35-day batch interval and no buffer room. In this case, the maximal fattening duration is reduced rela- tively to 7-day batch interval, and there is no possibility to extend the fattening period of light pigs. In the case of impaired health, it results in the delivery to the slaughter- house of a large proportion of light pigs.

The amounts of N and P excreted also showed high variability, resulting in high variability in the associated environmental impacts CC, AC and EU. However, for EU it is worth mentioning that health status had an effect on N excreted and EU, but not on P excreted. Impaired health resulted in higher FCR what means that for the same weight gain more feed was needed. Consequently, since all environmental impacts were calculated according to LCA, EU impact was degraded by impaired health status through the increase of emissions and resources needed for feed production.

The variability in model variables is similar to that observed in the French fattening pig unit database (IFIP, 2016) for FCR (2.76 + 0.08 V. 2.88 + 0.28 kg feed/kg gain), Lean (60.2 + 0.7 V. 60.3 + 0.9%), %ASW (91.4 + 9.8 V. 86.0 + 6.8%) and Rev (127.10 + 5.80 V. 125.00 + 11.90 €/pig). Differences are observed, however, between predicted and observed values for certain variables, such as SW (114.9 V. 120.9 + 4.1 kg) and GM (6.40 + 5.30 V. 16.60 + 15.80 €/pig), which can be explained by the greater diversity in batch management and economic context in the French database (e.g. batch interval, pig genotype, health status and label). In addition, 50% of model scenarios represented impaired health status, while results of the French database are rarely associated with health status. Furthermore, the DP feeding and individual scale of applica- tion simulated do not currently exist on commercial farms and thus do not exist in the French database.

Variability in performance among scenarios is due mainly to the health status of the population, followed by the batch interval and the feed sequence plan. In simulations, health status is represented by growth, FI potential and mortality rate. In a previous sensitivity analysis, the last two factors had the largest influence on model variables (Cadero et al., 2018b). With reduced FI and growth potential, pigs with impaired health have higher FCR and lower SW than healthy pigs for a given fattening duration, which results in lower revenue and gross margin per pig, which are also influ- enced by mortality. The healthy population produced more kg live weight than the impaired health population, which reduced its environmental impacts per kg live weight gain. The two batch intervals created different constraints for fatten- ing duration, which influences SA, SW and the %ASW. This difference in fattening duration results in differences in total N and P excreted and FC, because the longer the pigs remain on the farm, the more they eat and excrete. Daily-phase feed- ing closely follows the needs of each pig and thus results in less nutrient excess and excretion. Nitrogen excretion directly influences CC, AC and EU and indirectly influences CED,
which explains the significant contribution of feed sequence plan to the variability in these impacts.

While health status has a major effect on technical, economic and environmental performances of the unit, management practices can also significantly decrease or increase these effects. For instance, the influence of the batch interval differs depending on the health status. In the healthy population, classes with the 35-day batch interval have higher gross margin and lower environmental impacts than those with the 7-day interval for the same feeding practices. Conversely, in the impaired health population, classes with the 7-day batch interval have higher gross margin but higher environmental impacts than those with the 35-day interval. In the impaired health population, the 7-day batch interval yields a longer fattening duration than the 35-day interval, thus increasing %ASW. Vangroenweghe et al. (2012) studied effects of chang- ing from a 7-day batch interval to a 28- or 35-day interval on the health status of the herd. They observed that health status improved significantly as batch interval increased (7 V. 28 V. 35 days), perhaps because increasing the batch interval decreases the number of simultaneous batches on the farm and the mixing of pigs from different batches. Increasing the batch interval seems to improve the health status of the herd, and accounting for this effect in the model would have needed the development of a whole epidemiological sub-model like the one of Lurette et al. (2008).

In the healthy population, restricted instead of ad libitum feeding increased lean content by 1.5% and gross margin by 27.4% (3.60 €/pig), and reduced FCR by 1.5%, N excretion by 3.8% and all environmental impacts (CC, AC, EU, CED and LO) by 1.6%, on average. In the impaired health population, restricted V. ad libitum feeding increased lean content by 0.9% and gross margin by 67.5% (1.50 €/pig), and reduced FCR by 0.9%, N excretion by 2.5% and all environmental impacts (CC, AC, EU, CED and LO) by 0.9%, on average. Brossard et al. (2014) showed that using restricted V. ad libitum feeding decreased FCR by 1.1% and N excreted by 3.6%, and increased lean content by 1.6%. Therefore, with restricted feeding, pigs reach the target SW later than with ad libitum feeding. Depending on the batch interval and the use of a buffer room, this may or may not result in a decrease in SW.

For the feed sequence plan, using a DP V. 2P plan increased FCR. Although we formulated Feeds A and B to meet 110% of the average requirement of the gilts at 30 kg and 120 BW, respectively (Brossard et al., 2014), when increasing the number of feeding phases, some of the most demanding pigs do not have their requirements covered at the starting of each phase. This is even more pro- nounced for DP feeding. Using a DP V. 2P plan decreased N excretion by 11.8%; P excretion by 4.0%; CC, AC, EU, CED by 4.8% and LO by 1.7%, on average, in both populations. It also increased gross margin by 15.2% (1.90 €/pig) in the healthy population and by 84.0% (2.20 €/pig) in the impaired health population. Daily-phase feeding follows nutritional

requirements of pigs more closely and reduces oversupply of nutrients, as mentioned, especially of amino acids and minerals. Pomar et al. (2014) showed that DP feeding reduced N excretion by 11.8% and P excretion by 1.9% com- pared to three-phase feeding. Nitrogen excretion was reduced by 30% when DP feeding was applied at the individ- ual scale compared to three-phase feeding applied at the room scale (Andretta et al., 2016). In our virtual experiment, DP feeding reduced N excretion by 12% compared to 2P feeding, and DP feeding applied at the individual scale reduced N excretion by 15% compared to 2P feeding applied at the room scale. The difference between the decrease in N excretion found by Andretta et al. (2016) and our virtual experiment is because our feeds were formulated to meet at least 110% of the mean SID amino-acid requirements of the population, while those of Andretta et al. (2016) were formulated to meet 100% of SID amino-acid requirements of each individual.

Last, interaction between feeding strategies and health status also occurred. Indeed class with 35-day interval and DP with impaired health resulted in the same average environmental impact values as class with 7-day interval, 2P and ad libitum feeding for a healthy population (Figure 4). This means that adequate feeding strategies and animal management can compensate, to some extent, the effects of impaired health on environmental impacts. Regarding gross margin, on the contrary, all scenarios with impaired health resulted in lower value than in healthy population.

Modelling, a possible alternative to animal experiments Modelling is a recognized alternative to animal experiments, with lower costs and fewer ethical problems (Doke and Dhawale, 2015). Models that represent livestock farming sys- tems can help research practices improve economic and envi- ronmental performances of farms (Gouttenoire et al., 2011). Our study tested effects of 96 combinations of factors, which would have been difficult to perform in vivo, given the num- ber of pigs simulated (4400 or 21 200 per scenario, depend- ing on batch interval) and the time needed per scenario (at least 105 days in vivo V. 2 min with the model). The modelling approach also helps to understand mechanisms responsible for differences in farm performance.

Modelling also appears an interesting approach to explore interactions with animal health status, which is difficult to address in experiments. Health status of the herd directly influences technical performance (Pastorelli et al., 2012). Impaired health increases the energy required for maintenance and modifies requirements for amino acids (van der Meer et al., 2016). Therefore, the health status of the pig herd requires adapting feeding practices to better fit these changes. Variability in the health status of pigs on the farm increases the variability in the pigs' requirements. Our study shows that applying DP and/or restricted feeding at the individual scale decreases the effect of impaired health status on economic and environmental performances. However, we used results from a meta-analysis based on

experimental data to simulate the loss in pig performance for impaired health status, through the average effect of a single disease. The performance damage is usually much more severe in field conditions, as several challenges can apply simultaneously and have additional effects. The effects of health status in interaction with other factors may be more pronounced than in our simulations. Ultimately, adjusting nutrient supply as closely as possible to pig requirements seems an appropriate solution to increase a farm's income, regardless of the health status of its pig herd. This assertion is consistent with previous studies (Pomar et al., 2010, Andretta et al., 2016, Monteiro et al., 2017).
20: The HC separated the healthy population scenarios into eight classes, while based on the inertia gain between clustering steps, we retained six classes for the impaired health popu- lation scenarios (Table 2). For each population, classes differed mainly by batch interval and feed sequence plan, with a smaller discriminant effect of feed rationing plan (Figure 3). On average, and as expected, the healthy popu- lation had better technical, economic and environmental results than the impaired health population (Table 3). Classes' variabilities in GM, expressed as a percentage of mean GM of their scenarios, were large, ranging from 222% for the class with the highest mean GM (35-day batch interval, DP restricted feeding, 35dDPRes) to -9% for the class with the lowest mean GM (35-day batch interval, 2P feeding, 35d2P) (Figure 4A). Healthy population classes had higher mean GM than impaired health population classes.

Among healthy population classes, Res plans had higher mean GM than ad libitum plans. Within each feed rationing plan, classes with the DP sequence had higher mean GM than classes with the 2P sequence. Classes with the 35-day batch interval always had higher mean GM than classes with same feeding plan and the 7-day interval but signifi- cantly so only for the 2P-Res plan.

Among impaired health population classes, mean GM was the lowest for classes 7d2P and 35d2P, in which the feeding plan was applied at the Pen or Room scale. Among the other classes, mean GM was higher for classes with the 7-day batch interval than the 35-day interval.

Classes are ranked from highest to lowest mean gross margin. 35 days = 35-day interval between arrival of two successive batches on the farm; 7 days = 7-day interval between arrival of two successive batches on the farm; 2P = two-phase feed sequence plan; DP = daily-phase feed sequence plan; Res = restriction to 2.5 kg/day rationingplan;Adl=adlibitumplan;Ind=individualscaleat which thefeedingplanisapplied = R + P = scenarios with room or pen scale at which the feeding plan is applied.

(gross margin, percentages of pigs slaughtered at adequate SW or too light weight), which depend on many technical results and aggregate the variability in them. Indeed, gross margin is directly related to FC, which is influenced by the

feeding strategy, feed efficiency and revenue per pig, which are related to SW, carcass lean content and pig mortality. Percentage of pigs with a light SW in the payment grid had the highest variability because a group of 12 scenarios

was characterized by an average value of %LSW equal to 30%. These 12 scenarios corresponded to impaired health combined with 35-day batch interval and no buffer room. In this case, the maximal fattening duration is reduced rela- tively to 7-day batch interval, and there is no possibility to extend the fattening period of light pigs. In the case of impaired health, it results in the delivery to the slaughter- house of a large proportion of light pigs.

The amounts of N and P excreted also showed high variability, resulting in high variability in the associated environmental impacts CC, AC and EU. However, for EU it is worth mentioning that health status had an effect on N excreted and EU, but not on P excreted. Impaired health resulted in higher FCR what means that for the same weight gain more feed was needed. Consequently, since all environmental impacts were calculated according to LCA, EU impact was degraded by impaired health status through the increase of emissions and resources needed for feed production.

The variability in model variables is similar to that observed in the French fattening pig unit database (IFIP, 2016) for FCR (2.76 + 0.08 V. 2.88 + 0.28 kg feed/kg gain), Lean (60.2 + 0.7 V. 60.3 + 0.9%), %ASW (91.4 + 9.8 V. 86.0 + 6.8%) and Rev (127.10 + 5.80 V. 125.00 + 11.90 €/pig). Differences are observed, however, between predicted and observed values for certain variables, such as SW (114.9 V. 120.9 + 4.1 kg) and GM (6.40 + 5.30 V. 16.60 + 15.80 €/pig), which can be explained by the greater diversity in batch management and economic context in the French database (e.g. batch interval, pig genotype, health status and label). In addition, 50% of model scenarios represented impaired health status, while results of the French database are rarely associated with health status. Furthermore, the DP feeding and individual scale of applica- tion simulated do not currently exist on commercial farms and thus do not exist in the French database.

Variability in performance among scenarios is due mainly to the health status of the population, followed by the batch interval and the feed sequence plan. In simulations, health status is represented by growth, FI potential and mortality rate. In a previous sensitivity analysis, the last two factors had the largest influence on model variables (Cadero et al., 2018b). With reduced FI and growth potential, pigs with impaired health have higher FCR and lower SW than healthy pigs for a given fattening duration, which results in lower revenue and gross margin per pig, which are also influ- enced by mortality. The healthy population produced more kg live weight than the impaired health population, which reduced its environmental impacts per kg live weight gain. The two batch intervals created different constraints for fatten- ing duration, which influences SA, SW and the %ASW. This difference in fattening duration results in differences in total N and P excreted and FC, because the longer the pigs remain on the farm, the more they eat and excrete. Daily-phase feed- ing closely follows the needs of each pig and thus results in less nutrient excess and excretion. Nitrogen excretion directly influences CC, AC and EU and indirectly influences CED,
which explains the significant contribution of feed sequence plan to the variability in these impacts.

While health status has a major effect on technical, economic and environmental performances of the unit, management practices can also significantly decrease or increase these effects. For instance, the influence of the batch interval differs depending on the health status. In the healthy population, classes with the 35-day batch interval have higher gross margin and lower environmental impacts than those with the 7-day interval for the same feeding practices. Conversely, in the impaired health population, classes with the 7-day batch interval have higher gross margin but higher environmental impacts than those with the 35-day interval. In the impaired health population, the 7-day batch interval yields a longer fattening duration than the 35-day interval, thus increasing %ASW. Vangroenweghe et al. (2012) studied effects of chang- ing from a 7-day batch interval to a 28- or 35-day interval on the health status of the herd. They observed that health status improved significantly as batch interval increased (7 V. 28 V. 35 days), perhaps because increasing the batch interval decreases the number of simultaneous batches on the farm and the mixing of pigs from different batches. Increasing the batch interval seems to improve the health status of the herd, and accounting for this effect in the model would have needed the development of a whole epidemiological sub-model like the one of Lurette et al. (2008).

In the healthy population, restricted instead of ad libitum feeding increased lean content by 1.5% and gross margin by 27.4% (3.60 €/pig), and reduced FCR by 1.5%, N excretion by 3.8% and all environmental impacts (CC, AC, EU, CED and LO) by 1.6%, on average. In the impaired health population, restricted V. ad libitum feeding increased lean content by 0.9% and gross margin by 67.5% (1.50 €/pig), and reduced FCR by 0.9%, N excretion by 2.5% and all environmental impacts (CC, AC, EU, CED and LO) by 0.9%, on average. Brossard et al. (2014) showed that using restricted V. ad libitum feeding decreased FCR by 1.1% and N excreted by 3.6%, and increased lean content by 1.6%. Therefore, with restricted feeding, pigs reach the target SW later than with ad libitum feeding. Depending on the batch interval and the use of a buffer room, this may or may not result in a decrease in SW.

For the feed sequence plan, using a DP V. 2P plan increased FCR. Although we formulated Feeds A and B to meet 110% of the average requirement of the gilts at 30 kg and 120 BW, respectively (Brossard et al., 2014), when increasing the number of feeding phases, some of the most demanding pigs do not have their requirements covered at the starting of each phase. This is even more pro- nounced for DP feeding. Using a DP V. 2P plan decreased N excretion by 11.8%; P excretion by 4.0%; CC, AC, EU, CED by 4.8% and LO by 1.7%, on average, in both populations. It also increased gross margin by 15.2% (1.90 €/pig) in the healthy population and by 84.0% (2.20 €/pig) in the impaired health population. Daily-phase feeding follows nutritional

requirements of pigs more closely and reduces oversupply of nutrients, as mentioned, especially of amino acids and minerals. Pomar et al. (2014) showed that DP feeding reduced N excretion by 11.8% and P excretion by 1.9% com- pared to three-phase feeding. Nitrogen excretion was reduced by 30% when DP feeding was applied at the individ- ual scale compared to three-phase feeding applied at the room scale (Andretta et al., 2016). In our virtual experiment, DP feeding reduced N excretion by 12% compared to 2P feeding, and DP feeding applied at the individual scale reduced N excretion by 15% compared to 2P feeding applied at the room scale. The difference between the decrease in N excretion found by Andretta et al. (2016) and our virtual experiment is because our feeds were formulated to meet at least 110% of the mean SID amino-acid requirements of the population, while those of Andretta et al. (2016) were formulated to meet 100% of SID amino-acid requirements of each individual.

Last, interaction between feeding strategies and health status also occurred. Indeed class with 35-day interval and DP with impaired health resulted in the same average environmental impact values as class with 7-day interval, 2P and ad libitum feeding for a healthy population (Figure 4). This means that adequate feeding strategies and animal management can compensate, to some extent, the effects of impaired health on environmental impacts. Regarding gross margin, on the contrary, all scenarios with impaired health resulted in lower value than in healthy population.

Modelling, a possible alternative to animal experiments Modelling is a recognized alternative to animal experiments, with lower costs and fewer ethical problems (Doke and Dhawale, 2015). Models that represent livestock farming sys- tems can help research practices improve economic and envi- ronmental performances of farms (Gouttenoire et al., 2011). Our study tested effects of 96 combinations of factors, which would have been difficult to perform in vivo, given the num- ber of pigs simulated (4400 or 21 200 per scenario, depend- ing on batch interval) and the time needed per scenario (at least 105 days in vivo V. 2 min with the model). The modelling approach also helps to understand mechanisms responsible for differences in farm performance.

Modelling also appears an interesting approach to explore interactions with animal health status, which is difficult to address in experiments. Health status of the herd directly influences technical performance (Pastorelli et al., 2012). Impaired health increases the energy required for maintenance and modifies requirements for amino acids (van der Meer et al., 2016). Therefore, the health status of the pig herd requires adapting feeding practices to better fit these changes. Variability in the health status of pigs on the farm increases the variability in the pigs' requirements. Our study shows that applying DP and/or restricted feeding at the individual scale decreases the effect of impaired health status on economic and environmental performances. However, we used results from a meta-analysis based on

experimental data to simulate the loss in pig performance for impaired health status, through the average effect of a single disease. The performance damage is usually much more severe in field conditions, as several challenges can apply simultaneously and have additional effects. The effects of health status in interaction with other factors may be more pronounced than in our simulations. Ultimately, adjusting nutrient supply as closely as possible to pig requirements seems an appropriate solution to increase a farm's income, regardless of the health status of its pig herd. This assertion is consistent with previous studies (Pomar et al., 2010, Andretta et al., 2016, Monteiro et al., 2017).
21: The HC separated the healthy population scenarios into eight classes, while based on the inertia gain between clustering steps, we retained six classes for the impaired health popu- lation scenarios (Table 2). For each population, classes differed mainly by batch interval and feed sequence plan, with a smaller discriminant effect of feed rationing plan (Figure 3). On average, and as expected, the healthy popu- lation had better technical, economic and environmental results than the impaired health population (Table 3). Classes' variabilities in GM, expressed as a percentage of mean GM of their scenarios, were large, ranging from 222% for the class with the highest mean GM (35-day batch interval, DP restricted feeding, 35dDPRes) to -9% for the class with the lowest mean GM (35-day batch interval, 2P feeding, 35d2P) (Figure 4A). Healthy population classes had higher mean GM than impaired health population classes.

Among healthy population classes, Res plans had higher mean GM than ad libitum plans. Within each feed rationing plan, classes with the DP sequence had higher mean GM than classes with the 2P sequence. Classes with the 35-day batch interval always had higher mean GM than classes with same feeding plan and the 7-day interval but signifi- cantly so only for the 2P-Res plan.

Among impaired health population classes, mean GM was the lowest for classes 7d2P and 35d2P, in which the feeding plan was applied at the Pen or Room scale. Among the other classes, mean GM was higher for classes with the 7-day batch interval than the 35-day interval.
