Classes are ranked from highest to lowest mean gross margin. 35 days = 35-day interval between arrival of two successive batches on the farm; 7 days = 7-day interval between arrival of two successive batches on the farm; 2P = two-phase feed sequence plan; DP = daily-phase feed sequence plan; Res = restriction to 2.5 kg/day rationingplan;Adl=adlibitumplan;Ind=individualscaleat which thefeedingplanisapplied = R + P = scenarios with room or pen scale at which the feeding plan is applied.

(gross margin, percentages of pigs slaughtered at adequate SW or too light weight), which depend on many technical results and aggregate the variability in them. Indeed, gross margin is directly related to FC, which is influenced by the

feeding strategy, feed efficiency and revenue per pig, which are related to SW, carcass lean content and pig mortality. Percentage of pigs with a light SW in the payment grid had the highest variability because a group of 12 scenarios

was characterized by an average value of %LSW equal to 30%. These 12 scenarios corresponded to impaired health combined with 35-day batch interval and no buffer room. In this case, the maximal fattening duration is reduced rela- tively to 7-day batch interval, and there is no possibility to extend the fattening period of light pigs. In the case of impaired health, it results in the delivery to the slaughter- house of a large proportion of light pigs.

The amounts of N and P excreted also showed high variability, resulting in high variability in the associated environmental impacts CC, AC and EU. However, for EU it is worth mentioning that health status had an effect on N excreted and EU, but not on P excreted. Impaired health resulted in higher FCR what means that for the same weight gain more feed was needed. Consequently, since all environmental impacts were calculated according to LCA, EU impact was degraded by impaired health status through the increase of emissions and resources needed for feed production.

The variability in model variables is similar to that observed in the French fattening pig unit database (IFIP, 2016) for FCR (2.76 + 0.08 V. 2.88 + 0.28 kg feed/kg gain), Lean (60.2 + 0.7 V. 60.3 + 0.9%), %ASW (91.4 + 9.8 V. 86.0 + 6.8%) and Rev (127.10 + 5.80 V. 125.00 + 11.90 €/pig). Differences are observed, however, between predicted and observed values for certain variables, such as SW (114.9 V. 120.9 + 4.1 kg) and GM (6.40 + 5.30 V. 16.60 + 15.80 €/pig), which can be explained by the greater diversity in batch management and economic context in the French database (e.g. batch interval, pig genotype, health status and label). In addition, 50% of model scenarios represented impaired health status, while results of the French database are rarely associated with health status. Furthermore, the DP feeding and individual scale of applica- tion simulated do not currently exist on commercial farms and thus do not exist in the French database.

Variability in performance among scenarios is due mainly to the health status of the population, followed by the batch interval and the feed sequence plan. In simulations, health status is represented by growth, FI potential and mortality rate. In a previous sensitivity analysis, the last two factors had the largest influence on model variables (Cadero et al., 2018b). With reduced FI and growth potential, pigs with impaired health have higher FCR and lower SW than healthy pigs for a given fattening duration, which results in lower revenue and gross margin per pig, which are also influ- enced by mortality. The healthy population produced more kg live weight than the impaired health population, which reduced its environmental impacts per kg live weight gain. The two batch intervals created different constraints for fatten- ing duration, which influences SA, SW and the %ASW. This difference in fattening duration results in differences in total N and P excreted and FC, because the longer the pigs remain on the farm, the more they eat and excrete. Daily-phase feed- ing closely follows the needs of each pig and thus results in less nutrient excess and excretion. Nitrogen excretion directly influences CC, AC and EU and indirectly influences CED,
which explains the significant contribution of feed sequence plan to the variability in these impacts.

While health status has a major effect on technical, economic and environmental performances of the unit, management practices can also significantly decrease or increase these effects. For instance, the influence of the batch interval differs depending on the health status. In the healthy population, classes with the 35-day batch interval have higher gross margin and lower environmental impacts than those with the 7-day interval for the same feeding practices. Conversely, in the impaired health population, classes with the 7-day batch interval have higher gross margin but higher environmental impacts than those with the 35-day interval. In the impaired health population, the 7-day batch interval yields a longer fattening duration than the 35-day interval, thus increasing %ASW. Vangroenweghe et al. (2012) studied effects of chang- ing from a 7-day batch interval to a 28- or 35-day interval on the health status of the herd. They observed that health status improved significantly as batch interval increased (7 V. 28 V. 35 days), perhaps because increasing the batch interval decreases the number of simultaneous batches on the farm and the mixing of pigs from different batches. Increasing the batch interval seems to improve the health status of the herd, and accounting for this effect in the model would have needed the development of a whole epidemiological sub-model like the one of Lurette et al. (2008).

In the healthy population, restricted instead of ad libitum feeding increased lean content by 1.5% and gross margin by 27.4% (3.60 €/pig), and reduced FCR by 1.5%, N excretion by 3.8% and all environmental impacts (CC, AC, EU, CED and LO) by 1.6%, on average. In the impaired health population, restricted V. ad libitum feeding increased lean content by 0.9% and gross margin by 67.5% (1.50 €/pig), and reduced FCR by 0.9%, N excretion by 2.5% and all environmental impacts (CC, AC, EU, CED and LO) by 0.9%, on average. Brossard et al. (2014) showed that using restricted V. ad libitum feeding decreased FCR by 1.1% and N excreted by 3.6%, and increased lean content by 1.6%. Therefore, with restricted feeding, pigs reach the target SW later than with ad libitum feeding. Depending on the batch interval and the use of a buffer room, this may or may not result in a decrease in SW.

For the feed sequence plan, using a DP V. 2P plan increased FCR. Although we formulated Feeds A and B to meet 110% of the average requirement of the gilts at 30 kg and 120 BW, respectively (Brossard et al., 2014), when increasing the number of feeding phases, some of the most demanding pigs do not have their requirements covered at the starting of each phase. This is even more pro- nounced for DP feeding. Using a DP V. 2P plan decreased N excretion by 11.8%; P excretion by 4.0%; CC, AC, EU, CED by 4.8% and LO by 1.7%, on average, in both populations. It also increased gross margin by 15.2% (1.90 €/pig) in the healthy population and by 84.0% (2.20 €/pig) in the impaired health population. Daily-phase feeding follows nutritional

requirements of pigs more closely and reduces oversupply of nutrients, as mentioned, especially of amino acids and minerals. Pomar et al. (2014) showed that DP feeding reduced N excretion by 11.8% and P excretion by 1.9% com- pared to three-phase feeding. Nitrogen excretion was reduced by 30% when DP feeding was applied at the individ- ual scale compared to three-phase feeding applied at the room scale (Andretta et al., 2016). In our virtual experiment, DP feeding reduced N excretion by 12% compared to 2P feeding, and DP feeding applied at the individual scale reduced N excretion by 15% compared to 2P feeding applied at the room scale. The difference between the decrease in N excretion found by Andretta et al. (2016) and our virtual experiment is because our feeds were formulated to meet at least 110% of the mean SID amino-acid requirements of the population, while those of Andretta et al. (2016) were formulated to meet 100% of SID amino-acid requirements of each individual.

Last, interaction between feeding strategies and health status also occurred. Indeed class with 35-day interval and DP with impaired health resulted in the same average environmental impact values as class with 7-day interval, 2P and ad libitum feeding for a healthy population (Figure 4). This means that adequate feeding strategies and animal management can compensate, to some extent, the effects of impaired health on environmental impacts. Regarding gross margin, on the contrary, all scenarios with impaired health resulted in lower value than in healthy population.

Modelling, a possible alternative to animal experiments Modelling is a recognized alternative to animal experiments, with lower costs and fewer ethical problems (Doke and Dhawale, 2015). Models that represent livestock farming sys- tems can help research practices improve economic and envi- ronmental performances of farms (Gouttenoire et al., 2011). Our study tested effects of 96 combinations of factors, which would have been difficult to perform in vivo, given the num- ber of pigs simulated (4400 or 21 200 per scenario, depend- ing on batch interval) and the time needed per scenario (at least 105 days in vivo V. 2 min with the model). The modelling approach also helps to understand mechanisms responsible for differences in farm performance.

Modelling also appears an interesting approach to explore interactions with animal health status, which is difficult to address in experiments. Health status of the herd directly influences technical performance (Pastorelli et al., 2012). Impaired health increases the energy required for maintenance and modifies requirements for amino acids (van der Meer et al., 2016). Therefore, the health status of the pig herd requires adapting feeding practices to better fit these changes. Variability in the health status of pigs on the farm increases the variability in the pigs' requirements. Our study shows that applying DP and/or restricted feeding at the individual scale decreases the effect of impaired health status on economic and environmental performances. However, we used results from a meta-analysis based on

experimental data to simulate the loss in pig performance for impaired health status, through the average effect of a single disease. The performance damage is usually much more severe in field conditions, as several challenges can apply simultaneously and have additional effects. The effects of health status in interaction with other factors may be more pronounced than in our simulations. Ultimately, adjusting nutrient supply as closely as possible to pig requirements seems an appropriate solution to increase a farm's income, regardless of the health status of its pig herd. This assertion is consistent with previous studies (Pomar et al., 2010, Andretta et al., 2016, Monteiro et al., 2017).
28: The HC separated the healthy population scenarios into eight classes, while based on the inertia gain between clustering steps, we retained six classes for the impaired health popu- lation scenarios (Table 2). For each population, classes differed mainly by batch interval and feed sequence plan, with a smaller discriminant effect of feed rationing plan (Figure 3). On average, and as expected, the healthy popu- lation had better technical, economic and environmental results than the impaired health population (Table 3). Classes' variabilities in GM, expressed as a percentage of mean GM of their scenarios, were large, ranging from 222% for the class with the highest mean GM (35-day batch interval, DP restricted feeding, 35dDPRes) to -9% for the class with the lowest mean GM (35-day batch interval, 2P feeding, 35d2P) (Figure 4A). Healthy population classes had higher mean GM than impaired health population classes.

Among healthy population classes, Res plans had higher mean GM than ad libitum plans. Within each feed rationing plan, classes with the DP sequence had higher mean GM than classes with the 2P sequence. Classes with the 35-day batch interval always had higher mean GM than classes with same feeding plan and the 7-day interval but signifi- cantly so only for the 2P-Res plan.

Among impaired health population classes, mean GM was the lowest for classes 7d2P and 35d2P, in which the feeding plan was applied at the Pen or Room scale. Among the other classes, mean GM was higher for classes with the 7-day batch interval than the 35-day interval.

Classes are ranked from highest to lowest mean gross margin. 35 days = 35-day interval between arrival of two successive batches on the farm; 7 days = 7-day interval between arrival of two successive batches on the farm; 2P = two-phase feed sequence plan; DP = daily-phase feed sequence plan; Res = restriction to 2.5 kg/day rationingplan;Adl=adlibitumplan;Ind=individualscaleat which thefeedingplanisapplied = R + P = scenarios with room or pen scale at which the feeding plan is applied.

(gross margin, percentages of pigs slaughtered at adequate SW or too light weight), which depend on many technical results and aggregate the variability in them. Indeed, gross margin is directly related to FC, which is influenced by the

feeding strategy, feed efficiency and revenue per pig, which are related to SW, carcass lean content and pig mortality. Percentage of pigs with a light SW in the payment grid had the highest variability because a group of 12 scenarios

was characterized by an average value of %LSW equal to 30%. These 12 scenarios corresponded to impaired health combined with 35-day batch interval and no buffer room. In this case, the maximal fattening duration is reduced rela- tively to 7-day batch interval, and there is no possibility to extend the fattening period of light pigs. In the case of impaired health, it results in the delivery to the slaughter- house of a large proportion of light pigs.

The amounts of N and P excreted also showed high variability, resulting in high variability in the associated environmental impacts CC, AC and EU. However, for EU it is worth mentioning that health status had an effect on N excreted and EU, but not on P excreted. Impaired health resulted in higher FCR what means that for the same weight gain more feed was needed. Consequently, since all environmental impacts were calculated according to LCA, EU impact was degraded by impaired health status through the increase of emissions and resources needed for feed production.

The variability in model variables is similar to that observed in the French fattening pig unit database (IFIP, 2016) for FCR (2.76 + 0.08 V. 2.88 + 0.28 kg feed/kg gain), Lean (60.2 + 0.7 V. 60.3 + 0.9%), %ASW (91.4 + 9.8 V. 86.0 + 6.8%) and Rev (127.10 + 5.80 V. 125.00 + 11.90 €/pig). Differences are observed, however, between predicted and observed values for certain variables, such as SW (114.9 V. 120.9 + 4.1 kg) and GM (6.40 + 5.30 V. 16.60 + 15.80 €/pig), which can be explained by the greater diversity in batch management and economic context in the French database (e.g. batch interval, pig genotype, health status and label). In addition, 50% of model scenarios represented impaired health status, while results of the French database are rarely associated with health status. Furthermore, the DP feeding and individual scale of applica- tion simulated do not currently exist on commercial farms and thus do not exist in the French database.

Variability in performance among scenarios is due mainly to the health status of the population, followed by the batch interval and the feed sequence plan. In simulations, health status is represented by growth, FI potential and mortality rate. In a previous sensitivity analysis, the last two factors had the largest influence on model variables (Cadero et al., 2018b). With reduced FI and growth potential, pigs with impaired health have higher FCR and lower SW than healthy pigs for a given fattening duration, which results in lower revenue and gross margin per pig, which are also influ- enced by mortality. The healthy population produced more kg live weight than the impaired health population, which reduced its environmental impacts per kg live weight gain. The two batch intervals created different constraints for fatten- ing duration, which influences SA, SW and the %ASW. This difference in fattening duration results in differences in total N and P excreted and FC, because the longer the pigs remain on the farm, the more they eat and excrete. Daily-phase feed- ing closely follows the needs of each pig and thus results in less nutrient excess and excretion. Nitrogen excretion directly influences CC, AC and EU and indirectly influences CED,
which explains the significant contribution of feed sequence plan to the variability in these impacts.

While health status has a major effect on technical, economic and environmental performances of the unit, management practices can also significantly decrease or increase these effects. For instance, the influence of the batch interval differs depending on the health status. In the healthy population, classes with the 35-day batch interval have higher gross margin and lower environmental impacts than those with the 7-day interval for the same feeding practices. Conversely, in the impaired health population, classes with the 7-day batch interval have higher gross margin but higher environmental impacts than those with the 35-day interval. In the impaired health population, the 7-day batch interval yields a longer fattening duration than the 35-day interval, thus increasing %ASW. Vangroenweghe et al. (2012) studied effects of chang- ing from a 7-day batch interval to a 28- or 35-day interval on the health status of the herd. They observed that health status improved significantly as batch interval increased (7 V. 28 V. 35 days), perhaps because increasing the batch interval decreases the number of simultaneous batches on the farm and the mixing of pigs from different batches. Increasing the batch interval seems to improve the health status of the herd, and accounting for this effect in the model would have needed the development of a whole epidemiological sub-model like the one of Lurette et al. (2008).

In the healthy population, restricted instead of ad libitum feeding increased lean content by 1.5% and gross margin by 27.4% (3.60 €/pig), and reduced FCR by 1.5%, N excretion by 3.8% and all environmental impacts (CC, AC, EU, CED and LO) by 1.6%, on average. In the impaired health population, restricted V. ad libitum feeding increased lean content by 0.9% and gross margin by 67.5% (1.50 €/pig), and reduced FCR by 0.9%, N excretion by 2.5% and all environmental impacts (CC, AC, EU, CED and LO) by 0.9%, on average. Brossard et al. (2014) showed that using restricted V. ad libitum feeding decreased FCR by 1.1% and N excreted by 3.6%, and increased lean content by 1.6%. Therefore, with restricted feeding, pigs reach the target SW later than with ad libitum feeding. Depending on the batch interval and the use of a buffer room, this may or may not result in a decrease in SW.

For the feed sequence plan, using a DP V. 2P plan increased FCR. Although we formulated Feeds A and B to meet 110% of the average requirement of the gilts at 30 kg and 120 BW, respectively (Brossard et al., 2014), when increasing the number of feeding phases, some of the most demanding pigs do not have their requirements covered at the starting of each phase. This is even more pro- nounced for DP feeding. Using a DP V. 2P plan decreased N excretion by 11.8%; P excretion by 4.0%; CC, AC, EU, CED by 4.8% and LO by 1.7%, on average, in both populations. It also increased gross margin by 15.2% (1.90 €/pig) in the healthy population and by 84.0% (2.20 €/pig) in the impaired health population. Daily-phase feeding follows nutritional

requirements of pigs more closely and reduces oversupply of nutrients, as mentioned, especially of amino acids and minerals. Pomar et al. (2014) showed that DP feeding reduced N excretion by 11.8% and P excretion by 1.9% com- pared to three-phase feeding. Nitrogen excretion was reduced by 30% when DP feeding was applied at the individ- ual scale compared to three-phase feeding applied at the room scale (Andretta et al., 2016). In our virtual experiment, DP feeding reduced N excretion by 12% compared to 2P feeding, and DP feeding applied at the individual scale reduced N excretion by 15% compared to 2P feeding applied at the room scale. The difference between the decrease in N excretion found by Andretta et al. (2016) and our virtual experiment is because our feeds were formulated to meet at least 110% of the mean SID amino-acid requirements of the population, while those of Andretta et al. (2016) were formulated to meet 100% of SID amino-acid requirements of each individual.

Last, interaction between feeding strategies and health status also occurred. Indeed class with 35-day interval and DP with impaired health resulted in the same average environmental impact values as class with 7-day interval, 2P and ad libitum feeding for a healthy population (Figure 4). This means that adequate feeding strategies and animal management can compensate, to some extent, the effects of impaired health on environmental impacts. Regarding gross margin, on the contrary, all scenarios with impaired health resulted in lower value than in healthy population.

Modelling, a possible alternative to animal experiments Modelling is a recognized alternative to animal experiments, with lower costs and fewer ethical problems (Doke and Dhawale, 2015). Models that represent livestock farming sys- tems can help research practices improve economic and envi- ronmental performances of farms (Gouttenoire et al., 2011). Our study tested effects of 96 combinations of factors, which would have been difficult to perform in vivo, given the num- ber of pigs simulated (4400 or 21 200 per scenario, depend- ing on batch interval) and the time needed per scenario (at least 105 days in vivo V. 2 min with the model). The modelling approach also helps to understand mechanisms responsible for differences in farm performance.

Modelling also appears an interesting approach to explore interactions with animal health status, which is difficult to address in experiments. Health status of the herd directly influences technical performance (Pastorelli et al., 2012). Impaired health increases the energy required for maintenance and modifies requirements for amino acids (van der Meer et al., 2016). Therefore, the health status of the pig herd requires adapting feeding practices to better fit these changes. Variability in the health status of pigs on the farm increases the variability in the pigs' requirements. Our study shows that applying DP and/or restricted feeding at the individual scale decreases the effect of impaired health status on economic and environmental performances. However, we used results from a meta-analysis based on

experimental data to simulate the loss in pig performance for impaired health status, through the average effect of a single disease. The performance damage is usually much more severe in field conditions, as several challenges can apply simultaneously and have additional effects. The effects of health status in interaction with other factors may be more pronounced than in our simulations. Ultimately, adjusting nutrient supply as closely as possible to pig requirements seems an appropriate solution to increase a farm's income, regardless of the health status of its pig herd. This assertion is consistent with previous studies (Pomar et al., 2010, Andretta et al., 2016, Monteiro et al., 2017).
29: The HC separated the healthy population scenarios into eight classes, while based on the inertia gain between clustering steps, we retained six classes for the impaired health popu- lation scenarios (Table 2). For each population, classes differed mainly by batch interval and feed sequence plan, with a smaller discriminant effect of feed rationing plan (Figure 3). On average, and as expected, the healthy popu- lation had better technical, economic and environmental results than the impaired health population (Table 3). Classes' variabilities in GM, expressed as a percentage of mean GM of their scenarios, were large, ranging from 222% for the class with the highest mean GM (35-day batch interval, DP restricted feeding, 35dDPRes) to -9% for the class with the lowest mean GM (35-day batch interval, 2P feeding, 35d2P) (Figure 4A). Healthy population classes had higher mean GM than impaired health population classes.

Among healthy population classes, Res plans had higher mean GM than ad libitum plans. Within each feed rationing plan, classes with the DP sequence had higher mean GM than classes with the 2P sequence. Classes with the 35-day batch interval always had higher mean GM than classes with same feeding plan and the 7-day interval but signifi- cantly so only for the 2P-Res plan.

Among impaired health population classes, mean GM was the lowest for classes 7d2P and 35d2P, in which the feeding plan was applied at the Pen or Room scale. Among the other classes, mean GM was higher for classes with the 7-day batch interval than the 35-day interval.

(gross margin, percentages of pigs slaughtered at adequate SW or too light weight), which depend on many technical results and aggregate the variability in them. Indeed, gross margin is directly related to FC, which is influenced by the

feeding strategy, feed efficiency and revenue per pig, which are related to SW, carcass lean content and pig mortality. Percentage of pigs with a light SW in the payment grid had the highest variability because a group of 12 scenarios

was characterized by an average value of %LSW equal to 30%. These 12 scenarios corresponded to impaired health combined with 35-day batch interval and no buffer room. In this case, the maximal fattening duration is reduced rela- tively to 7-day batch interval, and there is no possibility to extend the fattening period of light pigs. In the case of impaired health, it results in the delivery to the slaughter- house of a large proportion of light pigs.

The amounts of N and P excreted also showed high variability, resulting in high variability in the associated environmental impacts CC, AC and EU. However, for EU it is worth mentioning that health status had an effect on N excreted and EU, but not on P excreted. Impaired health resulted in higher FCR what means that for the same weight gain more feed was needed. Consequently, since all environmental impacts were calculated according to LCA, EU impact was degraded by impaired health status through the increase of emissions and resources needed for feed production.

The variability in model variables is similar to that observed in the French fattening pig unit database (IFIP, 2016) for FCR (2.76 + 0.08 V. 2.88 + 0.28 kg feed/kg gain), Lean (60.2 + 0.7 V. 60.3 + 0.9%), %ASW (91.4 + 9.8 V. 86.0 + 6.8%) and Rev (127.10 + 5.80 V. 125.00 + 11.90 €/pig). Differences are observed, however, between predicted and observed values for certain variables, such as SW (114.9 V. 120.9 + 4.1 kg) and GM (6.40 + 5.30 V. 16.60 + 15.80 €/pig), which can be explained by the greater diversity in batch management and economic context in the French database (e.g. batch interval, pig genotype, health status and label). In addition, 50% of model scenarios represented impaired health status, while results of the French database are rarely associated with health status. Furthermore, the DP feeding and individual scale of applica- tion simulated do not currently exist on commercial farms and thus do not exist in the French database.

Variability in performance among scenarios is due mainly to the health status of the population, followed by the batch interval and the feed sequence plan. In simulations, health status is represented by growth, FI potential and mortality rate. In a previous sensitivity analysis, the last two factors had the largest influence on model variables (Cadero et al., 2018b). With reduced FI and growth potential, pigs with impaired health have higher FCR and lower SW than healthy pigs for a given fattening duration, which results in lower revenue and gross margin per pig, which are also influ- enced by mortality. The healthy population produced more kg live weight than the impaired health population, which reduced its environmental impacts per kg live weight gain. The two batch intervals created different constraints for fatten- ing duration, which influences SA, SW and the %ASW. This difference in fattening duration results in differences in total N and P excreted and FC, because the longer the pigs remain on the farm, the more they eat and excrete. Daily-phase feed- ing closely follows the needs of each pig and thus results in less nutrient excess and excretion. Nitrogen excretion directly influences CC, AC and EU and indirectly influences CED,
which explains the significant contribution of feed sequence plan to the variability in these impacts.

While health status has a major effect on technical, economic and environmental performances of the unit, management practices can also significantly decrease or increase these effects. For instance, the influence of the batch interval differs depending on the health status. In the healthy population, classes with the 35-day batch interval have higher gross margin and lower environmental impacts than those with the 7-day interval for the same feeding practices. Conversely, in the impaired health population, classes with the 7-day batch interval have higher gross margin but higher environmental impacts than those with the 35-day interval. In the impaired health population, the 7-day batch interval yields a longer fattening duration than the 35-day interval, thus increasing %ASW. Vangroenweghe et al. (2012) studied effects of chang- ing from a 7-day batch interval to a 28- or 35-day interval on the health status of the herd. They observed that health status improved significantly as batch interval increased (7 V. 28 V. 35 days), perhaps because increasing the batch interval decreases the number of simultaneous batches on the farm and the mixing of pigs from different batches. Increasing the batch interval seems to improve the health status of the herd, and accounting for this effect in the model would have needed the development of a whole epidemiological sub-model like the one of Lurette et al. (2008).

In the healthy population, restricted instead of ad libitum feeding increased lean content by 1.5% and gross margin by 27.4% (3.60 €/pig), and reduced FCR by 1.5%, N excretion by 3.8% and all environmental impacts (CC, AC, EU, CED and LO) by 1.6%, on average. In the impaired health population, restricted V. ad libitum feeding increased lean content by 0.9% and gross margin by 67.5% (1.50 €/pig), and reduced FCR by 0.9%, N excretion by 2.5% and all environmental impacts (CC, AC, EU, CED and LO) by 0.9%, on average. Brossard et al. (2014) showed that using restricted V. ad libitum feeding decreased FCR by 1.1% and N excreted by 3.6%, and increased lean content by 1.6%. Therefore, with restricted feeding, pigs reach the target SW later than with ad libitum feeding. Depending on the batch interval and the use of a buffer room, this may or may not result in a decrease in SW.

For the feed sequence plan, using a DP V. 2P plan increased FCR. Although we formulated Feeds A and B to meet 110% of the average requirement of the gilts at 30 kg and 120 BW, respectively (Brossard et al., 2014), when increasing the number of feeding phases, some of the most demanding pigs do not have their requirements covered at the starting of each phase. This is even more pro- nounced for DP feeding. Using a DP V. 2P plan decreased N excretion by 11.8%; P excretion by 4.0%; CC, AC, EU, CED by 4.8% and LO by 1.7%, on average, in both populations. It also increased gross margin by 15.2% (1.90 €/pig) in the healthy population and by 84.0% (2.20 €/pig) in the impaired health population. Daily-phase feeding follows nutritional

requirements of pigs more closely and reduces oversupply of nutrients, as mentioned, especially of amino acids and minerals. Pomar et al. (2014) showed that DP feeding reduced N excretion by 11.8% and P excretion by 1.9% com- pared to three-phase feeding. Nitrogen excretion was reduced by 30% when DP feeding was applied at the individ- ual scale compared to three-phase feeding applied at the room scale (Andretta et al., 2016). In our virtual experiment, DP feeding reduced N excretion by 12% compared to 2P feeding, and DP feeding applied at the individual scale reduced N excretion by 15% compared to 2P feeding applied at the room scale. The difference between the decrease in N excretion found by Andretta et al. (2016) and our virtual experiment is because our feeds were formulated to meet at least 110% of the mean SID amino-acid requirements of the population, while those of Andretta et al. (2016) were formulated to meet 100% of SID amino-acid requirements of each individual.

Last, interaction between feeding strategies and health status also occurred. Indeed class with 35-day interval and DP with impaired health resulted in the same average environmental impact values as class with 7-day interval, 2P and ad libitum feeding for a healthy population (Figure 4). This means that adequate feeding strategies and animal management can compensate, to some extent, the effects of impaired health on environmental impacts. Regarding gross margin, on the contrary, all scenarios with impaired health resulted in lower value than in healthy population.

Modelling, a possible alternative to animal experiments Modelling is a recognized alternative to animal experiments, with lower costs and fewer ethical problems (Doke and Dhawale, 2015). Models that represent livestock farming sys- tems can help research practices improve economic and envi- ronmental performances of farms (Gouttenoire et al., 2011). Our study tested effects of 96 combinations of factors, which would have been difficult to perform in vivo, given the num- ber of pigs simulated (4400 or 21 200 per scenario, depend- ing on batch interval) and the time needed per scenario (at least 105 days in vivo V. 2 min with the model). The modelling approach also helps to understand mechanisms responsible for differences in farm performance.

Modelling also appears an interesting approach to explore interactions with animal health status, which is difficult to address in experiments. Health status of the herd directly influences technical performance (Pastorelli et al., 2012). Impaired health increases the energy required for maintenance and modifies requirements for amino acids (van der Meer et al., 2016). Therefore, the health status of the pig herd requires adapting feeding practices to better fit these changes. Variability in the health status of pigs on the farm increases the variability in the pigs' requirements. Our study shows that applying DP and/or restricted feeding at the individual scale decreases the effect of impaired health status on economic and environmental performances. However, we used results from a meta-analysis based on

experimental data to simulate the loss in pig performance for impaired health status, through the average effect of a single disease. The performance damage is usually much more severe in field conditions, as several challenges can apply simultaneously and have additional effects. The effects of health status in interaction with other factors may be more pronounced than in our simulations. Ultimately, adjusting nutrient supply as closely as possible to pig requirements seems an appropriate solution to increase a farm's income, regardless of the health status of its pig herd. This assertion is consistent with previous studies (Pomar et al., 2010, Andretta et al., 2016, Monteiro et al., 2017).
30: The HC separated the healthy population scenarios into eight classes, while based on the inertia gain between clustering steps, we retained six classes for the impaired health popu- lation scenarios (Table 2). For each population, classes differed mainly by batch interval and feed sequence plan, with a smaller discriminant effect of feed rationing plan (Figure 3). On average, and as expected, the healthy popu- lation had better technical, economic and environmental results than the impaired health population (Table 3). Classes' variabilities in GM, expressed as a percentage of mean GM of their scenarios, were large, ranging from 222% for the class with the highest mean GM (35-day batch interval, DP restricted feeding, 35dDPRes) to -9% for the class with the lowest mean GM (35-day batch interval, 2P feeding, 35d2P) (Figure 4A). Healthy population classes had higher mean GM than impaired health population classes.

Among healthy population classes, Res plans had higher mean GM than ad libitum plans. Within each feed rationing plan, classes with the DP sequence had higher mean GM than classes with the 2P sequence. Classes with the 35-day batch interval always had higher mean GM than classes with same feeding plan and the 7-day interval but signifi- cantly so only for the 2P-Res plan.

Among impaired health population classes, mean GM was the lowest for classes 7d2P and 35d2P, in which the feeding plan was applied at the Pen or Room scale. Among the other classes, mean GM was higher for classes with the 7-day batch interval than the 35-day interval.

(gross margin, percentages of pigs slaughtered at adequate SW or too light weight), which depend on many technical results and aggregate the variability in them. Indeed, gross margin is directly related to FC, which is influenced by the

feeding strategy, feed efficiency and revenue per pig, which are related to SW, carcass lean content and pig mortality. Percentage of pigs with a light SW in the payment grid had the highest variability because a group of 12 scenarios

was characterized by an average value of %LSW equal to 30%. These 12 scenarios corresponded to impaired health combined with 35-day batch interval and no buffer room. In this case, the maximal fattening duration is reduced rela- tively to 7-day batch interval, and there is no possibility to extend the fattening period of light pigs. In the case of impaired health, it results in the delivery to the slaughter- house of a large proportion of light pigs.

The amounts of N and P excreted also showed high variability, resulting in high variability in the associated environmental impacts CC, AC and EU. However, for EU it is worth mentioning that health status had an effect on N excreted and EU, but not on P excreted. Impaired health resulted in higher FCR what means that for the same weight gain more feed was needed. Consequently, since all environmental impacts were calculated according to LCA, EU impact was degraded by impaired health status through the increase of emissions and resources needed for feed production.

The variability in model variables is similar to that observed in the French fattening pig unit database (IFIP, 2016) for FCR (2.76 + 0.08 V. 2.88 + 0.28 kg feed/kg gain), Lean (60.2 + 0.7 V. 60.3 + 0.9%), %ASW (91.4 + 9.8 V. 86.0 + 6.8%) and Rev (127.10 + 5.80 V. 125.00 + 11.90 €/pig). Differences are observed, however, between predicted and observed values for certain variables, such as SW (114.9 V. 120.9 + 4.1 kg) and GM (6.40 + 5.30 V. 16.60 + 15.80 €/pig), which can be explained by the greater diversity in batch management and economic context in the French database (e.g. batch interval, pig genotype, health status and label). In addition, 50% of model scenarios represented impaired health status, while results of the French database are rarely associated with health status. Furthermore, the DP feeding and individual scale of applica- tion simulated do not currently exist on commercial farms and thus do not exist in the French database.

Variability in performance among scenarios is due mainly to the health status of the population, followed by the batch interval and the feed sequence plan. In simulations, health status is represented by growth, FI potential and mortality rate. In a previous sensitivity analysis, the last two factors had the largest influence on model variables (Cadero et al., 2018b). With reduced FI and growth potential, pigs with impaired health have higher FCR and lower SW than healthy pigs for a given fattening duration, which results in lower revenue and gross margin per pig, which are also influ- enced by mortality. The healthy population produced more kg live weight than the impaired health population, which reduced its environmental impacts per kg live weight gain. The two batch intervals created different constraints for fatten- ing duration, which influences SA, SW and the %ASW. This difference in fattening duration results in differences in total N and P excreted and FC, because the longer the pigs remain on the farm, the more they eat and excrete. Daily-phase feed- ing closely follows the needs of each pig and thus results in less nutrient excess and excretion. Nitrogen excretion directly influences CC, AC and EU and indirectly influences CED,
which explains the significant contribution of feed sequence plan to the variability in these impacts.

While health status has a major effect on technical, economic and environmental performances of the unit, management practices can also significantly decrease or increase these effects. For instance, the influence of the batch interval differs depending on the health status. In the healthy population, classes with the 35-day batch interval have higher gross margin and lower environmental impacts than those with the 7-day interval for the same feeding practices. Conversely, in the impaired health population, classes with the 7-day batch interval have higher gross margin but higher environmental impacts than those with the 35-day interval. In the impaired health population, the 7-day batch interval yields a longer fattening duration than the 35-day interval, thus increasing %ASW. Vangroenweghe et al. (2012) studied effects of chang- ing from a 7-day batch interval to a 28- or 35-day interval on the health status of the herd. They observed that health status improved significantly as batch interval increased (7 V. 28 V. 35 days), perhaps because increasing the batch interval decreases the number of simultaneous batches on the farm and the mixing of pigs from different batches. Increasing the batch interval seems to improve the health status of the herd, and accounting for this effect in the model would have needed the development of a whole epidemiological sub-model like the one of Lurette et al. (2008).

In the healthy population, restricted instead of ad libitum feeding increased lean content by 1.5% and gross margin by 27.4% (3.60 €/pig), and reduced FCR by 1.5%, N excretion by 3.8% and all environmental impacts (CC, AC, EU, CED and LO) by 1.6%, on average. In the impaired health population, restricted V. ad libitum feeding increased lean content by 0.9% and gross margin by 67.5% (1.50 €/pig), and reduced FCR by 0.9%, N excretion by 2.5% and all environmental impacts (CC, AC, EU, CED and LO) by 0.9%, on average. Brossard et al. (2014) showed that using restricted V. ad libitum feeding decreased FCR by 1.1% and N excreted by 3.6%, and increased lean content by 1.6%. Therefore, with restricted feeding, pigs reach the target SW later than with ad libitum feeding. Depending on the batch interval and the use of a buffer room, this may or may not result in a decrease in SW.

For the feed sequence plan, using a DP V. 2P plan increased FCR. Although we formulated Feeds A and B to meet 110% of the average requirement of the gilts at 30 kg and 120 BW, respectively (Brossard et al., 2014), when increasing the number of feeding phases, some of the most demanding pigs do not have their requirements covered at the starting of each phase. This is even more pro- nounced for DP feeding. Using a DP V. 2P plan decreased N excretion by 11.8%; P excretion by 4.0%; CC, AC, EU, CED by 4.8% and LO by 1.7%, on average, in both populations. It also increased gross margin by 15.2% (1.90 €/pig) in the healthy population and by 84.0% (2.20 €/pig) in the impaired health population. Daily-phase feeding follows nutritional

requirements of pigs more closely and reduces oversupply of nutrients, as mentioned, especially of amino acids and minerals. Pomar et al. (2014) showed that DP feeding reduced N excretion by 11.8% and P excretion by 1.9% com- pared to three-phase feeding. Nitrogen excretion was reduced by 30% when DP feeding was applied at the individ- ual scale compared to three-phase feeding applied at the room scale (Andretta et al., 2016). In our virtual experiment, DP feeding reduced N excretion by 12% compared to 2P feeding, and DP feeding applied at the individual scale reduced N excretion by 15% compared to 2P feeding applied at the room scale. The difference between the decrease in N excretion found by Andretta et al. (2016) and our virtual experiment is because our feeds were formulated to meet at least 110% of the mean SID amino-acid requirements of the population, while those of Andretta et al. (2016) were formulated to meet 100% of SID amino-acid requirements of each individual.

Last, interaction between feeding strategies and health status also occurred. Indeed class with 35-day interval and DP with impaired health resulted in the same average environmental impact values as class with 7-day interval, 2P and ad libitum feeding for a healthy population (Figure 4). This means that adequate feeding strategies and animal management can compensate, to some extent, the effects of impaired health on environmental impacts. Regarding gross margin, on the contrary, all scenarios with impaired health resulted in lower value than in healthy population.

Modelling, a possible alternative to animal experiments Modelling is a recognized alternative to animal experiments, with lower costs and fewer ethical problems (Doke and Dhawale, 2015). Models that represent livestock farming sys- tems can help research practices improve economic and envi- ronmental performances of farms (Gouttenoire et al., 2011). Our study tested effects of 96 combinations of factors, which would have been difficult to perform in vivo, given the num- ber of pigs simulated (4400 or 21 200 per scenario, depend- ing on batch interval) and the time needed per scenario (at least 105 days in vivo V. 2 min with the model). The modelling approach also helps to understand mechanisms responsible for differences in farm performance.

Modelling also appears an interesting approach to explore interactions with animal health status, which is difficult to address in experiments. Health status of the herd directly influences technical performance (Pastorelli et al., 2012). Impaired health increases the energy required for maintenance and modifies requirements for amino acids (van der Meer et al., 2016). Therefore, the health status of the pig herd requires adapting feeding practices to better fit these changes. Variability in the health status of pigs on the farm increases the variability in the pigs' requirements. Our study shows that applying DP and/or restricted feeding at the individual scale decreases the effect of impaired health status on economic and environmental performances. However, we used results from a meta-analysis based on

experimental data to simulate the loss in pig performance for impaired health status, through the average effect of a single disease. The performance damage is usually much more severe in field conditions, as several challenges can apply simultaneously and have additional effects. The effects of health status in interaction with other factors may be more pronounced than in our simulations. Ultimately, adjusting nutrient supply as closely as possible to pig requirements seems an appropriate solution to increase a farm's income, regardless of the health status of its pig herd. This assertion is consistent with previous studies (Pomar et al., 2010, Andretta et al., 2016, Monteiro et al., 2017).
31: The HC separated the healthy population scenarios into eight classes, while based on the inertia gain between clustering steps, we retained six classes for the impaired health popu- lation scenarios (Table 2). For each population, classes differed mainly by batch interval and feed sequence plan, with a smaller discriminant effect of feed rationing plan (Figure 3). On average, and as expected, the healthy popu- lation had better technical, economic and environmental results than the impaired health population (Table 3). Classes' variabilities in GM, expressed as a percentage of mean GM of their scenarios, were large, ranging from 222% for the class with the highest mean GM (35-day batch interval, DP restricted feeding, 35dDPRes) to -9% for the class with the lowest mean GM (35-day batch interval, 2P feeding, 35d2P) (Figure 4A). Healthy population classes had higher mean GM than impaired health population classes.

Among healthy population classes, Res plans had higher mean GM than ad libitum plans. Within each feed rationing plan, classes with the DP sequence had higher mean GM than classes with the 2P sequence. Classes with the 35-day batch interval always had higher mean GM than classes with same feeding plan and the 7-day interval but signifi- cantly so only for the 2P-Res plan.

Among impaired health population classes, mean GM was the lowest for classes 7d2P and 35d2P, in which the feeding plan was applied at the Pen or Room scale. Among the other classes, mean GM was higher for classes with the 7-day batch interval than the 35-day interval.

(gross margin, percentages of pigs slaughtered at adequate SW or too light weight), which depend on many technical results and aggregate the variability in them. Indeed, gross margin is directly related to FC, which is influenced by the

feeding strategy, feed efficiency and revenue per pig, which are related to SW, carcass lean content and pig mortality. Percentage of pigs with a light SW in the payment grid had the highest variability because a group of 12 scenarios

was characterized by an average value of %LSW equal to 30%. These 12 scenarios corresponded to impaired health combined with 35-day batch interval and no buffer room. In this case, the maximal fattening duration is reduced rela- tively to 7-day batch interval, and there is no possibility to extend the fattening period of light pigs. In the case of impaired health, it results in the delivery to the slaughter- house of a large proportion of light pigs.

The amounts of N and P excreted also showed high variability, resulting in high variability in the associated environmental impacts CC, AC and EU. However, for EU it is worth mentioning that health status had an effect on N excreted and EU, but not on P excreted. Impaired health resulted in higher FCR what means that for the same weight gain more feed was needed. Consequently, since all environmental impacts were calculated according to LCA, EU impact was degraded by impaired health status through the increase of emissions and resources needed for feed production.

The variability in model variables is similar to that observed in the French fattening pig unit database (IFIP, 2016) for FCR (2.76 + 0.08 V. 2.88 + 0.28 kg feed/kg gain), Lean (60.2 + 0.7 V. 60.3 + 0.9%), %ASW (91.4 + 9.8 V. 86.0 + 6.8%) and Rev (127.10 + 5.80 V. 125.00 + 11.90 €/pig). Differences are observed, however, between predicted and observed values for certain variables, such as SW (114.9 V. 120.9 + 4.1 kg) and GM (6.40 + 5.30 V. 16.60 + 15.80 €/pig), which can be explained by the greater diversity in batch management and economic context in the French database (e.g. batch interval, pig genotype, health status and label). In addition, 50% of model scenarios represented impaired health status, while results of the French database are rarely associated with health status. Furthermore, the DP feeding and individual scale of applica- tion simulated do not currently exist on commercial farms and thus do not exist in the French database.

Variability in performance among scenarios is due mainly to the health status of the population, followed by the batch interval and the feed sequence plan. In simulations, health status is represented by growth, FI potential and mortality rate. In a previous sensitivity analysis, the last two factors had the largest influence on model variables (Cadero et al., 2018b). With reduced FI and growth potential, pigs with impaired health have higher FCR and lower SW than healthy pigs for a given fattening duration, which results in lower revenue and gross margin per pig, which are also influ- enced by mortality. The healthy population produced more kg live weight than the impaired health population, which reduced its environmental impacts per kg live weight gain. The two batch intervals created different constraints for fatten- ing duration, which influences SA, SW and the %ASW. This difference in fattening duration results in differences in total N and P excreted and FC, because the longer the pigs remain on the farm, the more they eat and excrete. Daily-phase feed- ing closely follows the needs of each pig and thus results in less nutrient excess and excretion. Nitrogen excretion directly influences CC, AC and EU and indirectly influences CED,
which explains the significant contribution of feed sequence plan to the variability in these impacts.

While health status has a major effect on technical, economic and environmental performances of the unit, management practices can also significantly decrease or increase these effects. For instance, the influence of the batch interval differs depending on the health status. In the healthy population, classes with the 35-day batch interval have higher gross margin and lower environmental impacts than those with the 7-day interval for the same feeding practices. Conversely, in the impaired health population, classes with the 7-day batch interval have higher gross margin but higher environmental impacts than those with the 35-day interval. In the impaired health population, the 7-day batch interval yields a longer fattening duration than the 35-day interval, thus increasing %ASW. Vangroenweghe et al. (2012) studied effects of chang- ing from a 7-day batch interval to a 28- or 35-day interval on the health status of the herd. They observed that health status improved significantly as batch interval increased (7 V. 28 V. 35 days), perhaps because increasing the batch interval decreases the number of simultaneous batches on the farm and the mixing of pigs from different batches. Increasing the batch interval seems to improve the health status of the herd, and accounting for this effect in the model would have needed the development of a whole epidemiological sub-model like the one of Lurette et al. (2008).

In the healthy population, restricted instead of ad libitum feeding increased lean content by 1.5% and gross margin by 27.4% (3.60 €/pig), and reduced FCR by 1.5%, N excretion by 3.8% and all environmental impacts (CC, AC, EU, CED and LO) by 1.6%, on average. In the impaired health population, restricted V. ad libitum feeding increased lean content by 0.9% and gross margin by 67.5% (1.50 €/pig), and reduced FCR by 0.9%, N excretion by 2.5% and all environmental impacts (CC, AC, EU, CED and LO) by 0.9%, on average. Brossard et al. (2014) showed that using restricted V. ad libitum feeding decreased FCR by 1.1% and N excreted by 3.6%, and increased lean content by 1.6%. Therefore, with restricted feeding, pigs reach the target SW later than with ad libitum feeding. Depending on the batch interval and the use of a buffer room, this may or may not result in a decrease in SW.

For the feed sequence plan, using a DP V. 2P plan increased FCR. Although we formulated Feeds A and B to meet 110% of the average requirement of the gilts at 30 kg and 120 BW, respectively (Brossard et al., 2014), when increasing the number of feeding phases, some of the most demanding pigs do not have their requirements covered at the starting of each phase. This is even more pro- nounced for DP feeding. Using a DP V. 2P plan decreased N excretion by 11.8%; P excretion by 4.0%; CC, AC, EU, CED by 4.8% and LO by 1.7%, on average, in both populations. It also increased gross margin by 15.2% (1.90 €/pig) in the healthy population and by 84.0% (2.20 €/pig) in the impaired health population. Daily-phase feeding follows nutritional

requirements of pigs more closely and reduces oversupply of nutrients, as mentioned, especially of amino acids and minerals. Pomar et al. (2014) showed that DP feeding reduced N excretion by 11.8% and P excretion by 1.9% com- pared to three-phase feeding. Nitrogen excretion was reduced by 30% when DP feeding was applied at the individ- ual scale compared to three-phase feeding applied at the room scale (Andretta et al., 2016). In our virtual experiment, DP feeding reduced N excretion by 12% compared to 2P feeding, and DP feeding applied at the individual scale reduced N excretion by 15% compared to 2P feeding applied at the room scale. The difference between the decrease in N excretion found by Andretta et al. (2016) and our virtual experiment is because our feeds were formulated to meet at least 110% of the mean SID amino-acid requirements of the population, while those of Andretta et al. (2016) were formulated to meet 100% of SID amino-acid requirements of each individual.

Last, interaction between feeding strategies and health status also occurred. Indeed class with 35-day interval and DP with impaired health resulted in the same average environmental impact values as class with 7-day interval, 2P and ad libitum feeding for a healthy population (Figure 4). This means that adequate feeding strategies and animal management can compensate, to some extent, the effects of impaired health on environmental impacts. Regarding gross margin, on the contrary, all scenarios with impaired health resulted in lower value than in healthy population.

Modelling, a possible alternative to animal experiments Modelling is a recognized alternative to animal experiments, with lower costs and fewer ethical problems (Doke and Dhawale, 2015). Models that represent livestock farming sys- tems can help research practices improve economic and envi- ronmental performances of farms (Gouttenoire et al., 2011). Our study tested effects of 96 combinations of factors, which would have been difficult to perform in vivo, given the num- ber of pigs simulated (4400 or 21 200 per scenario, depend- ing on batch interval) and the time needed per scenario (at least 105 days in vivo V. 2 min with the model). The modelling approach also helps to understand mechanisms responsible for differences in farm performance.

Modelling also appears an interesting approach to explore interactions with animal health status, which is difficult to address in experiments. Health status of the herd directly influences technical performance (Pastorelli et al., 2012). Impaired health increases the energy required for maintenance and modifies requirements for amino acids (van der Meer et al., 2016). Therefore, the health status of the pig herd requires adapting feeding practices to better fit these changes. Variability in the health status of pigs on the farm increases the variability in the pigs' requirements. Our study shows that applying DP and/or restricted feeding at the individual scale decreases the effect of impaired health status on economic and environmental performances. However, we used results from a meta-analysis based on

experimental data to simulate the loss in pig performance for impaired health status, through the average effect of a single disease. The performance damage is usually much more severe in field conditions, as several challenges can apply simultaneously and have additional effects. The effects of health status in interaction with other factors may be more pronounced than in our simulations. Ultimately, adjusting nutrient supply as closely as possible to pig requirements seems an appropriate solution to increase a farm's income, regardless of the health status of its pig herd. This assertion is consistent with previous studies (Pomar et al., 2010, Andretta et al., 2016, Monteiro et al., 2017).
32: The HC separated the healthy population scenarios into eight classes, while based on the inertia gain between clustering steps, we retained six classes for the impaired health popu- lation scenarios (Table 2). For each population, classes differed mainly by batch interval and feed sequence plan, with a smaller discriminant effect of feed rationing plan (Figure 3). On average, and as expected, the healthy popu- lation had better technical, economic and environmental results than the impaired health population (Table 3). Classes' variabilities in GM, expressed as a percentage of mean GM of their scenarios, were large, ranging from 222% for the class with the highest mean GM (35-day batch interval, DP restricted feeding, 35dDPRes) to -9% for the class with the lowest mean GM (35-day batch interval, 2P feeding, 35d2P) (Figure 4A). Healthy population classes had higher mean GM than impaired health population classes.

Among healthy population classes, Res plans had higher mean GM than ad libitum plans. Within each feed rationing plan, classes with the DP sequence had higher mean GM than classes with the 2P sequence. Classes with the 35-day batch interval always had higher mean GM than classes with same feeding plan and the 7-day interval but signifi- cantly so only for the 2P-Res plan.

Among impaired health population classes, mean GM was the lowest for classes 7d2P and 35d2P, in which the feeding plan was applied at the Pen or Room scale. Among the other classes, mean GM was higher for classes with the 7-day batch interval than the 35-day interval.

(gross margin, percentages of pigs slaughtered at adequate SW or too light weight), which depend on many technical results and aggregate the variability in them. Indeed, gross margin is directly related to FC, which is influenced by the

feeding strategy, feed efficiency and revenue per pig, which are related to SW, carcass lean content and pig mortality. Percentage of pigs with a light SW in the payment grid had the highest variability because a group of 12 scenarios

was characterized by an average value of %LSW equal to 30%. These 12 scenarios corresponded to impaired health combined with 35-day batch interval and no buffer room. In this case, the maximal fattening duration is reduced rela- tively to 7-day batch interval, and there is no possibility to extend the fattening period of light pigs. In the case of impaired health, it results in the delivery to the slaughter- house of a large proportion of light pigs.

The amounts of N and P excreted also showed high variability, resulting in high variability in the associated environmental impacts CC, AC and EU. However, for EU it is worth mentioning that health status had an effect on N excreted and EU, but not on P excreted. Impaired health resulted in higher FCR what means that for the same weight gain more feed was needed. Consequently, since all environmental impacts were calculated according to LCA, EU impact was degraded by impaired health status through the increase of emissions and resources needed for feed production.

The variability in model variables is similar to that observed in the French fattening pig unit database (IFIP, 2016) for FCR (2.76 + 0.08 V. 2.88 + 0.28 kg feed/kg gain), Lean (60.2 + 0.7 V. 60.3 + 0.9%), %ASW (91.4 + 9.8 V. 86.0 + 6.8%) and Rev (127.10 + 5.80 V. 125.00 + 11.90 €/pig). Differences are observed, however, between predicted and observed values for certain variables, such as SW (114.9 V. 120.9 + 4.1 kg) and GM (6.40 + 5.30 V. 16.60 + 15.80 €/pig), which can be explained by the greater diversity in batch management and economic context in the French database (e.g. batch interval, pig genotype, health status and label). In addition, 50% of model scenarios represented impaired health status, while results of the French database are rarely associated with health status. Furthermore, the DP feeding and individual scale of applica- tion simulated do not currently exist on commercial farms and thus do not exist in the French database.

Variability in performance among scenarios is due mainly to the health status of the population, followed by the batch interval and the feed sequence plan. In simulations, health status is represented by growth, FI potential and mortality rate. In a previous sensitivity analysis, the last two factors had the largest influence on model variables (Cadero et al., 2018b). With reduced FI and growth potential, pigs with impaired health have higher FCR and lower SW than healthy pigs for a given fattening duration, which results in lower revenue and gross margin per pig, which are also influ- enced by mortality. The healthy population produced more kg live weight than the impaired health population, which reduced its environmental impacts per kg live weight gain. The two batch intervals created different constraints for fatten- ing duration, which influences SA, SW and the %ASW. This difference in fattening duration results in differences in total N and P excreted and FC, because the longer the pigs remain on the farm, the more they eat and excrete. Daily-phase feed- ing closely follows the needs of each pig and thus results in less nutrient excess and excretion. Nitrogen excretion directly influences CC, AC and EU and indirectly influences CED,
which explains the significant contribution of feed sequence plan to the variability in these impacts.

While health status has a major effect on technical, economic and environmental performances of the unit, management practices can also significantly decrease or increase these effects. For instance, the influence of the batch interval differs depending on the health status. In the healthy population, classes with the 35-day batch interval have higher gross margin and lower environmental impacts than those with the 7-day interval for the same feeding practices. Conversely, in the impaired health population, classes with the 7-day batch interval have higher gross margin but higher environmental impacts than those with the 35-day interval. In the impaired health population, the 7-day batch interval yields a longer fattening duration than the 35-day interval, thus increasing %ASW. Vangroenweghe et al. (2012) studied effects of chang- ing from a 7-day batch interval to a 28- or 35-day interval on the health status of the herd. They observed that health status improved significantly as batch interval increased (7 V. 28 V. 35 days), perhaps because increasing the batch interval decreases the number of simultaneous batches on the farm and the mixing of pigs from different batches. Increasing the batch interval seems to improve the health status of the herd, and accounting for this effect in the model would have needed the development of a whole epidemiological sub-model like the one of Lurette et al. (2008).

In the healthy population, restricted instead of ad libitum feeding increased lean content by 1.5% and gross margin by 27.4% (3.60 €/pig), and reduced FCR by 1.5%, N excretion by 3.8% and all environmental impacts (CC, AC, EU, CED and LO) by 1.6%, on average. In the impaired health population, restricted V. ad libitum feeding increased lean content by 0.9% and gross margin by 67.5% (1.50 €/pig), and reduced FCR by 0.9%, N excretion by 2.5% and all environmental impacts (CC, AC, EU, CED and LO) by 0.9%, on average. Brossard et al. (2014) showed that using restricted V. ad libitum feeding decreased FCR by 1.1% and N excreted by 3.6%, and increased lean content by 1.6%. Therefore, with restricted feeding, pigs reach the target SW later than with ad libitum feeding. Depending on the batch interval and the use of a buffer room, this may or may not result in a decrease in SW.

For the feed sequence plan, using a DP V. 2P plan increased FCR. Although we formulated Feeds A and B to meet 110% of the average requirement of the gilts at 30 kg and 120 BW, respectively (Brossard et al., 2014), when increasing the number of feeding phases, some of the most demanding pigs do not have their requirements covered at the starting of each phase. This is even more pro- nounced for DP feeding. Using a DP V. 2P plan decreased N excretion by 11.8%; P excretion by 4.0%; CC, AC, EU, CED by 4.8% and LO by 1.7%, on average, in both populations. It also increased gross margin by 15.2% (1.90 €/pig) in the healthy population and by 84.0% (2.20 €/pig) in the impaired health population. Daily-phase feeding follows nutritional

requirements of pigs more closely and reduces oversupply of nutrients, as mentioned, especially of amino acids and minerals. Pomar et al. (2014) showed that DP feeding reduced N excretion by 11.8% and P excretion by 1.9% com- pared to three-phase feeding. Nitrogen excretion was reduced by 30% when DP feeding was applied at the individ- ual scale compared to three-phase feeding applied at the room scale (Andretta et al., 2016). In our virtual experiment, DP feeding reduced N excretion by 12% compared to 2P feeding, and DP feeding applied at the individual scale reduced N excretion by 15% compared to 2P feeding applied at the room scale. The difference between the decrease in N excretion found by Andretta et al. (2016) and our virtual experiment is because our feeds were formulated to meet at least 110% of the mean SID amino-acid requirements of the population, while those of Andretta et al. (2016) were formulated to meet 100% of SID amino-acid requirements of each individual.

Last, interaction between feeding strategies and health status also occurred. Indeed class with 35-day interval and DP with impaired health resulted in the same average environmental impact values as class with 7-day interval, 2P and ad libitum feeding for a healthy population (Figure 4). This means that adequate feeding strategies and animal management can compensate, to some extent, the effects of impaired health on environmental impacts. Regarding gross margin, on the contrary, all scenarios with impaired health resulted in lower value than in healthy population.

Modelling, a possible alternative to animal experiments Modelling is a recognized alternative to animal experiments, with lower costs and fewer ethical problems (Doke and Dhawale, 2015). Models that represent livestock farming sys- tems can help research practices improve economic and envi- ronmental performances of farms (Gouttenoire et al., 2011). Our study tested effects of 96 combinations of factors, which would have been difficult to perform in vivo, given the num- ber of pigs simulated (4400 or 21 200 per scenario, depend- ing on batch interval) and the time needed per scenario (at least 105 days in vivo V. 2 min with the model). The modelling approach also helps to understand mechanisms responsible for differences in farm performance.

Modelling also appears an interesting approach to explore interactions with animal health status, which is difficult to address in experiments. Health status of the herd directly influences technical performance (Pastorelli et al., 2012). Impaired health increases the energy required for maintenance and modifies requirements for amino acids (van der Meer et al., 2016). Therefore, the health status of the pig herd requires adapting feeding practices to better fit these changes. Variability in the health status of pigs on the farm increases the variability in the pigs' requirements. Our study shows that applying DP and/or restricted feeding at the individual scale decreases the effect of impaired health status on economic and environmental performances. However, we used results from a meta-analysis based on

experimental data to simulate the loss in pig performance for impaired health status, through the average effect of a single disease. The performance damage is usually much more severe in field conditions, as several challenges can apply simultaneously and have additional effects. The effects of health status in interaction with other factors may be more pronounced than in our simulations. Ultimately, adjusting nutrient supply as closely as possible to pig requirements seems an appropriate solution to increase a farm's income, regardless of the health status of its pig herd. This assertion is consistent with previous studies (Pomar et al., 2010, Andretta et al., 2016, Monteiro et al., 2017).
33: The HC separated the healthy population scenarios into eight classes, while based on the inertia gain between clustering steps, we retained six classes for the impaired health popu- lation scenarios (Table 2). For each population, classes differed mainly by batch interval and feed sequence plan, with a smaller discriminant effect of feed rationing plan (Figure 3). On average, and as expected, the healthy popu- lation had better technical, economic and environmental results than the impaired health population (Table 3). Classes' variabilities in GM, expressed as a percentage of mean GM of their scenarios, were large, ranging from 222% for the class with the highest mean GM (35-day batch interval, DP restricted feeding, 35dDPRes) to -9% for the class with the lowest mean GM (35-day batch interval, 2P feeding, 35d2P) (Figure 4A). Healthy population classes had higher mean GM than impaired health population classes.

Among healthy population classes, Res plans had higher mean GM than ad libitum plans. Within each feed rationing plan, classes with the DP sequence had higher mean GM than classes with the 2P sequence. Classes with the 35-day batch interval always had higher mean GM than classes with same feeding plan and the 7-day interval but signifi- cantly so only for the 2P-Res plan.

Among impaired health population classes, mean GM was the lowest for classes 7d2P and 35d2P, in which the feeding plan was applied at the Pen or Room scale. Among the other classes, mean GM was higher for classes with the 7-day batch interval than the 35-day interval.
